verses.text: In this thing may Yahweh pardon your servant: when my master goes into the house of Rimmon to worship there, and he leans on my hand, and I bow myself in the house of Rimmon. When I bow myself in the house of Rimmon, may Yahweh pardon your servant in this thing.”
verses.explainer: Naaman asks pardon for one thing: when he accompanies the king of Syria to bow in the temple of Rimmon, may the LORD forgive him -- a candid confession of the practical tension between new faith and old obligations.

thenVsNow.then: Naaman's dilemma -- genuine faith constrained by social and professional obligations to participate in systems that conflict with that faith -- is remarkably contemporary.
thenVsNow.now: Naaman's dilemma -- genuine faith constrained by social and professional obligations to participate in systems that conflict with that faith -- is remarkably contemporary.

verses.text:
He said to him, “Go in peace.”
So he departed from him a little way.
verses.explainer: Elisha says 'Go in peace' -- neither explicitly approving nor condemning the compromise, but recognizing the genuineness of Naaman's faith within its limitations.

verses.text: But Gehazi the servant of Elisha the man of God, said, “Behold, my master has spared this Naaman the Syrian, in not receiving at his hands that which he brought. As Yahweh lives, I will run after him, and take something from him.”
verses.explainer: Gehazi, Elisha's servant, decides to chase Naaman and take some of the refused gifts -- greed overrides the spiritual principle his master demonstrated.

verses.text: So Gehazi followed after Naaman. When Naaman saw one running after him, he came down from the chariot to meet him, and said, “Is all well?”
verses.explainer: Gehazi runs after Naaman, who steps down from his chariot to meet him -- the general's courtesy toward the servant contrasts with the servant's deception toward the general.

verses.text: He said, “All is well. My master has sent me, saying, ‘Behold, even now two young men of the sons of the prophets have come to me from the hill country of Ephraim. Please give them a talent of silver and two changes of clothing.’”
verses.explainer: Gehazi lies, claiming Elisha sent him because two prophets have arrived needing silver and clothing -- using his master's name to legitimize his greed.

thenVsNow.then: Using spiritual authority or a respected leader's name to enrich oneself is a pattern that disgraces communities of faith in every generation.
thenVsNow.now: Using spiritual authority or a respected leader's name to enrich oneself is a pattern that disgraces communities of faith in every generation.

verses.text: Naaman said, “Be pleased to take two talents.” He urged him, and bound two talents of silver in two bags, with two changes of clothing, and laid them on two of his servants; and they carried them before him.
verses.explainer: Naaman generously gives Gehazi even more than he asked -- two talents of silver and two changes of clothing, carried by two servants. The general's gratitude makes the deception worse.

verses.text: When he came to the hill, he took them from their hand, and stored them in the house. Then he let the men go, and they departed.
verses.explainer: Gehazi takes the gifts and hides them in his house, then returns to Elisha as if nothing happened -- attempting to deceive the prophet who has demonstrated supernatural knowledge repeatedly.

verses.text:
But he went in, and stood before his master. Elisha said to him, “Where did you come from, Gehazi?”
He said, “Your servant went nowhere.”
verses.explainer: Elisha asks 'Where have you been, Gehazi?' and Gehazi lies: 'Nowhere' -- the servant attempts to deceive the man through whom God has performed resurrection, multiplication, and healing.

verses.text: He said to him, “Didn’t my heart go with you, when the man turned from his chariot to meet you? Is it a time to receive money, and to receive garments, and olive groves and vineyards, and sheep and cattle, and male servants and female servants?
verses.explainer: Elisha reveals he saw everything in his spirit: 'Was not my heart there when the man turned from his chariot?' -- the prophet's spiritual perception makes deception impossible.

verses.text:
Therefore the leprosy of Naaman will cling to you and to your offspring forever.”
He went out from his presence a leper, as white as snow.
verses.explainer: Elisha pronounces Naaman's leprosy on Gehazi and his descendants forever, and Gehazi leaves covered in leprosy -- the greed that took what grace freely offered results in receiving the very condition grace had healed.

crossReferences.connection: Ananias and Sapphira's similar deception about money within the early church, with similarly severe consequences

thenVsNow.then: Gehazi's fate warns that attempting to profit from God's free grace brings severe consequences. The contrast between Naaman's healed leprosy and Gehazi's acquired leprosy illustrates the difference between humble faith and corrupt opportunism.
thenVsNow.now: Gehazi's fate warns that attempting to profit from God's free grace brings severe consequences. The contrast between Naaman's healed leprosy and Gehazi's acquired leprosy illustrates the difference between humble faith and corrupt opportunism.

explainer.summary: Three episodes demonstrate God's power through Elisha: a borrowed axe head floats, the Syrian army is struck blind and led captive into Samaria, and during Syria's siege of Samaria, Elisha reveals the invisible army of God protecting him. The chapter culminates with a horrifying famine siege where mothers are eating their children.
themes: God's invisible army protecting His servants
Prophetic intelligence in warfare
The horrors of siege warfare
God's care for small and great needs alike
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter transitions from Elisha's personal miracles to the larger political crisis between Israel and Syria. The siege of Samaria brings the northern kingdom to its most desperate point, setting up the miraculous deliverance in chapter 7.

wordStudies.greek: Hebrew: סוּסִים וְרֶכֶב אֵשׁ (susim v'rekhev esh)
wordStudies.translatedAs: The angelic army surrounding Elisha, visible only
wordStudies.meaning: The angelic army surrounding Elisha, visible only to those whose eyes God opens. The same fiery chariots that took Elijah to heaven now protect Elisha on earth -- God's heavenly military force operates continuously, whether seen or not.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To open eyes, both physically and spiritually
wordStudies.meaning: To open eyes, both physically and spiritually. Used for the servant's spiritual sight being opened to see the angelic army and for the Syrians' physical sight being restored. The wordplay connects physical and spiritual perception.

verses.text: The sons of the prophets said to Elisha, “See now, the place where we live and meet with you is too small for us.
verses.explainer: The prophetic community tells Elisha their meeting place is too small -- the growing number of prophets is a sign of renewal, suggesting Elisha's ministry is expanding the prophetic movement.

verses.text:
Please let us go to the Jordan, and each man take a beam from there, and let us make us a place there, where we may live.”
He answered, “Go!”
verses.explainer: They propose building a larger place by the Jordan, and Elisha agrees to go with them -- the prophet participates in community life, not standing above practical needs.

thenVsNow.then: Spiritual communities grow and need larger spaces. The prophet's willingness to join in manual labor shows that spiritual authority does not exempt one from practical work.
thenVsNow.now: Spiritual communities grow and need larger spaces. The prophet's willingness to join in manual labor shows that spiritual authority does not exempt one from practical work.

verses.text:
One said, “Please be pleased to go with your servants.”
He answered, “I will go.”
verses.explainer: One prophet asks Elisha to come along, and he agrees -- personal involvement in the community's work rather than directing from a distance.

verses.text: So he went with them. When they came to the Jordan, they cut down wood.
verses.explainer: They go to the Jordan and begin cutting trees for the new building -- prophetic communities engaging in physical labor, building their own facilities.

verses.text: But as one was cutting down a tree, the ax head fell into the water. Then he cried, and said, “Alas, my master! For it was borrowed.”
verses.explainer: An axe head falls into the water, and the prophet cries out because it was borrowed -- the loss of a borrowed tool was a serious matter when iron was expensive and the prophets were poor.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Iron tools were expensive in ancient Israel. A borrowed axe head represented a significant financial obligation. The prophetic communities' poverty meant even this small loss was devastating.

verses.text: The man of God asked, “Where did it fall?” He showed him the place. He cut down a stick, threw it in there, and made the iron float.
verses.explainer: Elisha cuts a stick and throws it in the water, and the axe head floats -- a miracle for a small, everyday need, showing that God cares about the practical concerns of ordinary people.

verses.text: He said, “Take it.” So he put out his hand and took it.
verses.explainer: Elisha tells the man to pick it up, and he reaches out and takes it -- the miracle requires the human response of faith, reaching out to claim what God has provided.

verses.text: Now the king of Syria was at war against Israel; and he took counsel with his servants, saying, “My camp will be in such and such a place.”
verses.explainer: The king of Syria is making war against Israel, and God reveals the Syrian plans to Elisha, who warns Israel's king -- prophetic intelligence gathering that consistently thwarts Syrian military strategy.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Elisha functions as a one-man intelligence agency, providing supernatural knowledge of enemy movements. This military application of prophetic gifts made him more valuable to Israel's defense than an army.

verses.text: The man of God sent to the king of Israel, saying, “Beware that you not pass this place; for the Syrians are coming down there.”
verses.explainer: Elisha repeatedly warns the king of Israel about Syrian ambush locations, saving him multiple times -- consistent, reliable prophetic guidance that becomes a strategic asset.

verses.text: The king of Israel sent to the place which the man of God told him and warned him of; and he saved himself there, not once or twice.
verses.explainer: The king of Israel sends scouts to the locations Elisha identifies, repeatedly avoiding Syrian traps -- the practical integration of prophetic revelation with military intelligence.

verses.text: The king of Syria’s heart was very troubled about this. He called his servants, and said to them, “Won’t you show me which of us is for the king of Israel?”
verses.explainer: The Syrian king is furious, suspecting a spy in his inner circle -- the consistent leak of military plans can only be explained by either treachery or supernatural intelligence.

verses.text: One of his servants said, “No, my lord, O king; but Elisha, the prophet who is in Israel, tells the king of Israel the words that you speak in your bedroom.”
verses.explainer: A Syrian officer reveals that Elisha the prophet tells the king of Israel the very words the Syrian king speaks in his bedroom -- the prophet's knowledge extends even to private conversations.

thenVsNow.then: The idea that no space is truly private before God -- that even whispered conversations in bedrooms are known -- challenges assumptions about the limits of divine awareness.
thenVsNow.now: The idea that no space is truly private before God -- that even whispered conversations in bedrooms are known -- challenges assumptions about the limits of divine awareness.

verses.text:
He said, “Go and see where he is, that I may send and get him.”
He was told, “Behold, he is in Dothan.”
verses.explainer: The Syrian king sends a great army with horses and chariots to Dothan to capture Elisha -- sending a military expedition against one prophet shows how seriously they take his threat.

verses.text: Therefore he sent horses, chariots, and a great army there. They came by night, and surrounded the city.
verses.explainer: The Syrian army arrives by night and surrounds Dothan -- a nighttime approach to prevent warning, treating the capture of one prophet as a major military operation.

verses.text: When the servant of the man of God had risen early, and gone out, behold, an army with horses and chariots was around the city. His servant said to him, “Alas, my master! What shall we do?”
verses.explainer: Elisha's servant rises early and sees the city surrounded by the enemy army -- panic is the natural response to being trapped by an overwhelming military force.

verses.text: He answered, “Don’t be afraid; for those who are with us are more than those who are with them.”
verses.explainer: Elisha says 'Do not fear, for those who are with us are more than those who are with them' -- one of the most faith-filled statements in Scripture, spoken by someone who sees what others cannot.

crossReferences.connection: Paul's declaration 'If God is for us, who can be against us?' echoes Elisha's confidence in the unseen reality

thenVsNow.then: Elisha's calm assurance that the invisible reality is more significant than the visible threat challenges a worldview that only counts what can be seen and measured.
thenVsNow.now: Elisha's calm assurance that the invisible reality is more significant than the visible threat challenges a worldview that only counts what can be seen and measured.

verses.text: Elisha prayed, and said, “Yahweh, please open his eyes, that he may see.” Yahweh opened the young man’s eyes; and he saw: and behold, the mountain was full of horses and chariots of fire around Elisha.
verses.explainer: Elisha prays for God to open the servant's eyes, and he sees the mountain full of horses and chariots of fire surrounding Elisha -- the invisible divine army that is always present, usually unseen.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The vision of the fiery army suggests that the spiritual battle behind visible events involves forces far greater than any human military. This concept of spiritual warfare behind physical reality is foundational to biblical worldview.

crossReferences.connection: The same chariots of fire that took Elijah now protect Elisha

verses.text:
When they came down to him, Elisha prayed to Yahweh, and said, “Please strike this people with blindness.”
He struck them with blindness according to Elishah’s word.
verses.explainer: When the Syrians advance, Elisha prays and God strikes them with blindness -- the same God who opened the servant's eyes now closes the enemy's eyes.

verses.text: Elisha said to them, “This is not the way, neither is this the city. Follow me, and I will bring you to the man whom you seek.” He led them to Samaria.
verses.explainer: Elisha tells the blinded soldiers 'This is not the right road or city; follow me' and leads them to Samaria -- a bold, almost comic deception that brings the enemy army into Israel's capital city.

thenVsNow.then: Elisha's strategy turns a military threat into an opportunity for mercy. Instead of destroying the enemy, he leads them to a place where they can be shown grace.
thenVsNow.now: Elisha's strategy turns a military threat into an opportunity for mercy. Instead of destroying the enemy, he leads them to a place where they can be shown grace.

verses.text:
When they had come into Samaria, Elisha said, “Yahweh, open these men’s eyes, that they may see.”
Yahweh opened their eyes, and they saw; and behold, they were in the middle of Samaria.
verses.explainer: In Samaria, Elisha prays and their eyes are opened -- they find themselves trapped in the middle of Israel's capital, completely at their enemy's mercy.

verses.text: The king of Israel said to Elisha, when he saw them, “My father, shall I strike them? Shall I strike them?”
verses.explainer: Israel's king eagerly asks Elisha 'Shall I kill them?' -- the military instinct is to slaughter captured enemies, especially those who came to kidnap the prophet.

verses.text: He answered, “You shall not strike them. Would you strike those whom you have taken captive with your sword and with your bow? Set bread and water before them, that they may eat and drink, and go to their master.”
verses.explainer: Elisha commands that they be fed and sent home, not killed -- radical mercy that transforms enemies into guests, ending the Syrian raids against Israel.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: In the ancient Near East, captured enemy soldiers were typically enslaved or executed. Elisha's command to feed them and release them was extraordinarily countercultural, anticipating Jesus' teaching to love enemies (Matt 5:44).

crossReferences.connection: Paul's instruction to feed a hungry enemy, quoting Proverbs 25:21-22, puts into practice the principle Elisha demonstrates here

thenVsNow.then: Elisha's strategy of mercy over vengeance accomplished more than military force: the Syrian raids stopped. Kindness can be more effective than violence in resolving conflict.
thenVsNow.now: Elisha's strategy of mercy over vengeance accomplished more than military force: the Syrian raids stopped. Kindness can be more effective than violence in resolving conflict.

verses.text: He prepared a great feast for them. When they had eaten and drunk, he sent them away, and they went to their master. So the bands of Syria stopped raiding the land of Israel.
verses.explainer: The king prepares a great feast for the Syrians and sends them home, and the Syrian raiding bands stop coming into Israel -- mercy achieves what military force could not: peace.

verses.text: After this, Benhadad king of Syria gathered all his army, and went up and besieged Samaria.
verses.explainer: Later, Ben-hadad of Syria besieges Samaria with his entire army -- a full-scale siege, different from the earlier raids, that brings Samaria to the brink of starvation.

verses.text: There was a great famine in Samaria. Behold, they besieged it, until a donkey’s head was sold for eighty pieces of silver, and the fourth part of a kab of dove’s dung for five pieces of silver.
verses.explainer: Famine becomes so severe that a donkey's head costs eighty shekels of silver and dove's dung costs five shekels -- hyperinflation of worthless food items showing complete economic collapse.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Siege warfare aimed to starve cities into submission. Without modern logistics, a besieged city consumed its food stores and eventually resorted to eating anything available -- including unclean animals and worse.

verses.text: As the king of Israel was passing by on the wall, a woman cried to him, saying, “Help, my lord, O king!”
verses.explainer: A woman cries to the king for help as he walks on the city wall -- the king's inspection of defenses brings him face to face with civilian suffering.

verses.text: He said, “If Yahweh doesn’t help you, where could I get help for you? From of the threshing floor, or from the wine press?”
verses.explainer: The king responds bitterly: 'If the LORD does not help you, how can I?' -- acknowledging both his helplessness and implicitly blaming God for the siege.

verses.text:
The king said to her, “What is your problem?”
She answered, “This woman said to me, ‘Give your son, that we may eat him today, and we will eat my son tomorrow.’
verses.explainer: The woman tells the king about an agreement to eat their children: they ate her son yesterday and now the other woman has hidden her son -- the horror of famine-driven cannibalism.

crossReferences.connection: Moses' warning that siege would drive Israel to cannibalism -- the most extreme covenant curse, now fulfilled

verses.text: So we boiled my son, and ate him: and I said to her on the next day, ‘Give your son, that we may eat him;’ and she has hidden her son.”
verses.explainer: They boiled and ate her son, but the next day the other woman hid her child -- even in mutual desperation, self-interest reasserts itself in the most grotesque circumstances.

verses.text: When the king heard the words of the woman, he tore his clothes. Now he was passing by on the wall, and the people looked, and behold, he had sackcloth underneath on his body.
verses.explainer: When the king hears this, he tears his clothes, revealing sackcloth underneath -- the king has been secretly mourning and fasting while maintaining a public facade of composure.

verses.text: Then he said, “God do so to me, and more also, if the head of Elisha the son of Shaphat stays on him today.”
verses.explainer: The king swears to behead Elisha that day -- blaming the prophet for the siege, perhaps believing Elisha could end it but refuses, or that his prayers have failed.

thenVsNow.then: Blaming spiritual leaders for crises they did not cause is a common human response. The king's rage at Elisha mirrors the tendency to shoot the messenger.
thenVsNow.now: Blaming spiritual leaders for crises they did not cause is a common human response. The king's rage at Elisha mirrors the tendency to shoot the messenger.

verses.text: But Elisha was sitting in his house, and the elders were sitting with him. Then the king sent a man from before him; but before the messenger came to him, he said to the elders, “Do you see how this son of a murderer has sent to take away my head? Behold, when the messenger comes, shut the door, and hold the door shut against him. Isn’t the sound of his master’s feet behind him?”
verses.explainer: Elisha, sitting with the elders, perceives the executioner coming before he arrives and tells them to hold the door -- prophetic foreknowledge provides practical self-defense.

verses.text: While he was still talking with them, behold, the messenger came down to him. Then he said, “Behold, this evil is from Yahweh. Why should I wait for Yahweh any longer?”
verses.explainer: The king's messenger arrives (some texts suggest the king himself follows), saying 'This trouble is from the LORD; why should I wait for the LORD any longer?' -- the king's theological despair reaches its lowest point.

explainer.summary: Elisha predicts that food prices will return to normal within twenty-four hours. Four lepers outside the city gates, with nothing to lose, discover that the Syrian army has fled -- God caused them to hear the sound of a vast army, and they abandoned everything. The lepers report the news, the city is saved, and the skeptical officer who doubted Elisha's prophecy is trampled to death in the stampede.
themes: Deliverance from impossible circumstances
The outcast as agent of salvation
Judgment on unbelief
God's supernatural intervention without human military action
explainer.contextInBook: After the horrifying siege conditions of chapter 6, deliverance comes through the most unlikely agents: four lepers who have nothing to lose. The pattern of God using the marginalized to accomplish His purposes runs throughout the Elisha narrative.

wordStudies.translatedAs: People afflicted with skin disease who were exclud
wordStudies.meaning: People afflicted with skin disease who were excluded from the community and lived outside the city gates. Their marginal status made them the perfect agents of discovery -- they had nothing to lose by approaching the enemy camp.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A dry measure of approximately 7 quarts
wordStudies.meaning: A dry measure of approximately 7 quarts. Elisha's prediction of a seah of flour for a shekel represents a return to normal prices from the hyperinflated famine prices, a complete economic reversal within twenty-four hours.

verses.text: Elisha said, “Hear Yahweh’s word. Yahweh says, ‘Tomorrow about this time a seah of fine flour will be sold for a shekel, and two seahs of barley for a shekel, in the gate of Samaria.’”
verses.explainer: Elisha prophesies that by tomorrow a measure of fine flour will sell for a shekel and two measures of barley for a shekel at the gate of Samaria -- a complete reversal of the famine economy within twenty-four hours.

verses.text:
Then the captain on whose hand the king leaned answered the man of God, and said, “Behold, if Yahweh made windows in heaven, could this thing be?”
He said, “Behold, you will see it with your eyes, but will not eat of it.”
verses.explainer: The king's officer scoffs: 'Even if the LORD made windows in heaven, could this happen?' and Elisha replies he will see it but not eat of it -- skepticism is answered with specific prophecy of personal judgment.

thenVsNow.then: The officer's skepticism, while understandable given the circumstances, demonstrates that doubting God's specific promise carries consequences. His fate becomes a warning about dismissing prophetic words.
thenVsNow.now: The officer's skepticism, while understandable given the circumstances, demonstrates that doubting God's specific promise carries consequences. His fate becomes a warning about dismissing prophetic words.

verses.text: Now there were four leprous men at the entrance of the gate. They said to one another, “Why do we sit here until we die?
verses.explainer: Four lepers at the city gate reason that they will die whether they stay or enter the city, so they might as well try the Syrian camp -- desperation produces a logic that privilege would never generate.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Lepers lived outside city gates, in a social no-man's-land. They were already dead to society, which paradoxically freed them to take the risk that would save the entire city.

verses.text: If we say, ‘We will enter into the city,’ then the famine is in the city, and we will die there. If we sit still here, we also die. Now therefore come, and let us surrender to the army of the Syrians. If they save us alive, we will live; and if they kill us, we will only die.”
verses.explainer: They calculate: if the Syrians spare them, they live; if the Syrians kill them, they were going to die anyway -- a rational risk assessment from the margin of society.

thenVsNow.then: The lepers' logic illustrates how desperate circumstances can produce clarity of thought. When you have nothing to lose, you can take risks that those with something to protect never would.
thenVsNow.now: The lepers' logic illustrates how desperate circumstances can produce clarity of thought. When you have nothing to lose, you can take risks that those with something to protect never would.

verses.text: They rose up in the twilight, to go to the camp of the Syrians. When they had come to the outermost part of the camp of the Syrians, behold, no man was there.
verses.explainer: They go to the Syrian camp at twilight and find it completely deserted -- the army has vanished, leaving everything behind.

verses.text: For the Lord had made the army of the Syrians to hear the sound of chariots, and the sound of horses, even the noise of a great army; and they said to one another, “Behold, the king of Israel has hired against us the kings of the Hittites and the kings of the Egyptians to attack us.”
verses.explainer: The LORD had caused the Syrian army to hear the sound of a great army approaching -- chariots, horses, a massive force -- and they panicked and fled, thinking Egypt and the Hittites had been hired against them.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: God used psychological warfare, not military force, to end the siege. The Syrians heard sounds their fears interpreted as the most devastating possible threat. No Israelite soldier was involved in the victory.

verses.text: Therefore they arose and fled in the twilight, and left their tents, and their horses, and their donkeys, even the camp as it was, and fled for their life.
verses.explainer: The Syrians abandon everything: tents, horses, donkeys, and the entire camp -- total, panicked rout without a single battle, leaving all their supplies behind.

verses.text: When these lepers came to the outermost part of the camp, they went into one tent, and ate and drink, and carried away silver, gold, and clothing, and went and hid it. Then they came back, and entered into another tent, and carried things from there also, and went and hid them.
verses.explainer: The lepers enter the first tent, eat, drink, and carry away silver, gold, and clothing to hide -- their initial response is personal enrichment, very human and completely understandable.

verses.text: Then they said to one another, “We aren’t doing right. Today is a day of good news, and we keep silent. If we wait until the morning light, punishment will overtake us. Now therefore come, let’s go and tell the king’s household.”
verses.explainer: They check themselves: 'We are not doing right. This is a day of good news and we are keeping silent' -- conscience overrides self-interest as they realize the starving city needs this news.

thenVsNow.then: The lepers' self-correction -- recognizing that good news carries an obligation to share it -- is a powerful metaphor for the responsibility of those who have received grace.
thenVsNow.now: The lepers' self-correction -- recognizing that good news carries an obligation to share it -- is a powerful metaphor for the responsibility of those who have received grace.

verses.text: So they came and called to the city gatekeepers; and they told them, “We came to the camp of the Syrians, and, behold, there was no man there, not even a man’s voice, but the horses tied, and the donkeys tied, and the tents as they were.”
verses.explainer: They report to the city gatekeepers that the Syrian camp is abandoned -- the lepers become the heralds of salvation, bringing news that saves the city.

verses.text: He called the gatekeepers; and they told it to the king’s household within.
verses.explainer: The gatekeepers call out the news and it reaches the king's household -- the chain of communication from the most marginalized members of society reaches the highest authority.

verses.text: The king arose in the night, and said to his servants, “I will now show you what the Syrians have done to us. They know that we are hungry. Therefore are they gone out of the camp to hide themselves in the field, saying, ‘When they come out of the city, we shall take them alive, and get into the city.’”
verses.explainer: The king suspects a Syrian trap: they have hidden to ambush when Israel comes out -- a reasonable military concern but one that nearly prevents the city from receiving its deliverance.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The king's suspicion of a ruse was tactically sound. Feigned retreats were common in ancient warfare. Yet his caution nearly blocked the deliverance God had already accomplished.

verses.text: One of his servants answered, “Please let some people take five of the horses that remain, which are left in the city. Behold, they are like all the multitude of Israel who are left in it. Behold, they are like all the multitude of Israel who are consumed. Let us send and see.”
verses.explainer: One of the king's servants suggests sending scouts with a few remaining horses to investigate -- wisdom suggests a cautious verification rather than either charging out or staying in.

verses.text: Therefore they took two chariots with horses; and the king sent them out to the Syrian army, saying, “Go and see.”
verses.explainer: They send two chariots after the Syrian army and discover they fled all the way back to the Jordan, dropping equipment along the entire route -- confirming a genuine, panic-driven rout.

verses.text: They went after them to the Jordan; and behold, all the path was full of garments and equipment which the Syrians had cast away in their haste. The messengers returned, and told the king.
verses.explainer: The messengers return confirming the Syrian army is gone, with discarded garments and equipment littering the road -- proof that the flight was real and total.

verses.text: The people went out and plundered the camp of the Syrians. So a seah of fine flour was sold for a shekel, and two measures of barley for a shekel, according to Yahweh’s word.
verses.explainer: The people rush out and plunder the Syrian camp, and food prices drop to exactly what Elisha prophesied -- a seah of flour for a shekel and two seahs of barley for a shekel.

crossReferences.connection: The exact fulfillment of Elisha's prophecy about food prices, down to the specific measures and prices

verses.text: The king appointed the captain on whose hand he leaned to be in charge of the gate; and the people trampled over him in the gate, and he died as the man of God had said, who spoke when the king came down to him.
verses.explainer: The king appoints the skeptical officer to manage the gate, and the crowd tramples him to death in the stampede -- the man who said this could not happen sees it happen but does not benefit from it.

crossReferences.connection: Elisha's prophecy that the officer would see the deliverance but not eat of it, fulfilled precisely

verses.text: It happened as the man of God had spoken to the king, saying, “Two seahs of barley for a shekel, and a seah of fine flour for a shekel, shall be tomorrow about this time in the gate of Samaria”;
verses.explainer: The narrator confirms this happened exactly as Elisha prophesied when the king came to him -- editorial confirmation that prophetic words are reliable and specific.

verses.text: and that captain answered the man of God, and said, “Now, behold, if Yahweh made windows in heaven, might such a thing be?” and he said, “Behold, you will see it with your eyes, but will not eat of it.”
verses.explainer: The officer's exact words of doubt are repeated, along with Elisha's response that he would see but not taste the abundance -- the repetition emphasizes that the officer's fate was sealed by his skepticism.

thenVsNow.then: The officer's fate illustrates a sobering principle: it is possible to witness God's deliverance firsthand and yet not participate in it because of unbelief.
thenVsNow.now: The officer's fate illustrates a sobering principle: it is possible to witness God's deliverance firsthand and yet not participate in it because of unbelief.

verses.text: It happened like that to him; for the people trampled over him in the gate, and he died.
verses.explainer: It happened exactly as prophesied: the people trampled the officer at the gate and he died -- the final confirmation that Elisha's word was completely accurate in every detail.

explainer.summary: The Shunammite woman's land is restored through providential timing. Elisha visits Damascus and weeps as he reveals to Hazael the atrocities Hazael will commit against Israel. Hazael assassinates Ben-hadad and becomes king. The chapter shifts to Judah, summarizing the reigns of Jehoram and Ahaziah -- both corrupted by marriage alliances with Ahab's house.
themes: Providence restoring what was lost
Prophetic grief over future violence
The corrupting influence of intermarriage with idolaters
God's patience with the Davidic line
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter serves as a transition, resolving the Shunammite story, fulfilling one of Elijah's commissions (Hazael), and showing how Ahab's influence has corrupted even Judah's royal line through intermarriage.

wordStudies.meaning: To weep or cry. Elisha weeps when he foresees Hazael's future cruelties against Israel. The prophet's tears reveal God's own grief over the violence that divine judgment requires -- judgment is God's strange work, not His delight.

An insulting term of self-deprecation. Hazael calls himself a 'dead dog' to express incredulity at the prophecy that he will become king and conquer Israel -- false humility from a man who will soon murder his way to the throne.

verses.text: Now Elisha had spoken to the woman whose son he had restored to life, saying, “Arise, and go, you and your household, and stay for a while wherever you can; for Yahweh has called for a famine. It will also come on the land for seven years.”
verses.explainer: Elisha warns the Shunammite woman to leave the land because a seven-year famine is coming -- the prophet provides advance warning that allows his benefactress to escape the coming disaster.

verses.text: The woman arose, and did according to the man of God’s word. She went with her household, and lived in the land of the Philistines for seven years.
verses.explainer: The woman obeys and lives in Philistine territory for seven years -- her trust in Elisha's word leads her to uproot her entire life for nearly a decade.

verses.text: At the end of seven years, the woman returned from the land of the Philistines. Then she went out to beg the king for her house and for her land.
verses.explainer: After the famine, she returns and appeals to the king for the restoration of her house and land -- property rights after long absence had to be adjudicated by the king.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: After seven years, her property had likely been occupied by others. Royal adjudication was needed to restore her rights, making the timing of her appeal critically important.

verses.text: Now the king was talking with Gehazi the servant of the man of God, saying, “Please tell me all the great things that Elisha has done.”
verses.explainer: At that exact moment, the king is asking Gehazi about Elisha's miracles -- the providential timing that brings together the king's curiosity and the woman's need.

verses.text: As he was telling the king how he had restored to life him who was dead, behold, the woman, whose son he had restored to life, begged the king for her house and for her land. Gehazi said, “My lord, O king, this is the woman, and this is her son, whom Elisha restored to life.”
verses.explainer: As Gehazi is telling the king about the Shunammite's son being raised, she arrives seeking her land -- the coincidence is so perfect it can only be divine orchestration.

thenVsNow.then: The providential timing -- arriving at the exact moment her story is being told -- illustrates how God orchestrates circumstances to accomplish His purposes through what appears to be coincidence.
thenVsNow.now: The providential timing -- arriving at the exact moment her story is being told -- illustrates how God orchestrates circumstances to accomplish His purposes through what appears to be coincidence.

verses.text: When the king asked the woman, she told him. So the king appointed to her a certain officer, saying, “Restore all that was hers, and all the fruits of the field since the day that she left the land, even until now.”
verses.explainer: The king restores all her property plus all the produce of her fields from the seven years of absence -- full restitution including back income, a generous royal ruling.

verses.text: Elisha came to Damascus; and Benhadad the king of Syria was sick. He was told, “The man of God has come here.”
verses.explainer: Elisha goes to Damascus, and Ben-hadad the king of Syria is sick. He sends Hazael to ask the prophet if he will recover -- even foreign kings seek Israel's prophet for divine information.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: That a Syrian king consults an Israelite prophet demonstrates Elisha's international reputation. Unlike Ahaziah who sought Baal-zebub (ch.1), even pagans recognize Yahweh's prophet as authoritative.

verses.text: The king said to Hazael, “Take a present in your hand, and go, meet the man of God, and inquire of Yahweh by him, saying, ‘Will I recover from this sickness?’”
verses.explainer: Ben-hadad sends Hazael with a massive gift -- forty camel-loads of Damascus's finest goods -- to inquire of Elisha.

verses.text: So Hazael went to meet him, and took a present with him, even of every good thing of Damascus, forty camels’ burden, and came and stood before him, and said, “Your son Benhadad king of Syria has sent me to you, saying, ‘Will I recover from this sickness?’”
verses.explainer: Hazael meets Elisha and presents himself as 'your son Ben-hadad' asking whether the king will recover -- the deferential language places the Syrian king in a subordinate position to God's prophet.

verses.text: Elisha said to him, “Go, tell him, ‘You will surely recover;’ however Yahweh has shown me that he will surely die.”
verses.explainer: Elisha gives a deliberately ambiguous answer: 'Go say to him, You shall certainly recover; but the LORD has shown me that he shall certainly die' -- the king's illness is not fatal, but he will die nonetheless.

verses.text: He settled his gaze steadfastly on him, until he was ashamed. Then the man of God wept.
verses.explainer: Elisha stares at Hazael until Hazael is uncomfortable, and then the man of God weeps -- the prophet sees the future horrors Hazael will inflict on Israel.

verses.text:
Hazael said, “Why do you weep, my lord?”
He answered, “Because I know the evil that you will do to the children of Israel. You will set their strongholds on fire, and you will kill their young men with the sword, and will dash their little ones in pieces, and rip up their pregnant women.”
verses.explainer: Elisha explains his tears: Hazael will burn Israel's strongholds, kill young men, dash children, and rip open pregnant women -- the catalogue of atrocities that ancient Near Eastern warfare commonly inflicted.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: These atrocities were standard practices of ancient Near Eastern warfare, documented in Assyrian and other records. Elisha does not predict unique cruelty but the ordinary horrors of war that Hazael will inflict on Israel.

thenVsNow.then: The prophet weeps over violence that has not yet occurred, illustrating divine grief over human suffering -- even when that suffering is the consequence of judgment on sin.
thenVsNow.now: The prophet weeps over violence that has not yet occurred, illustrating divine grief over human suffering -- even when that suffering is the consequence of judgment on sin.

verses.text:
Hazael said, “But what is your servant, who is but a dog, that he could do this great thing?”
Elisha answered, “Yahweh has shown me that you will be king over Syria.”
verses.explainer: Hazael feigns incredulity: 'What is your servant, who is but a dog, that he should do this great thing?' -- false humility from a man who is already calculating how to seize the throne.

verses.text:
Then he departed from Elisha, and came to his master, who said to him, “What did Elisha say to you?”
He answered, “He told me that you would surely recover.”
verses.explainer: Hazael returns and lies to Ben-hadad, saying 'He told me you would certainly recover' -- reporting only half of Elisha's message, concealing the prophecy of death to maintain the king's trust.

verses.text: On the next day, he took a thick cloth, dipped it in water, and spread it on his face, so that he died. Then Hazael reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: The next day Hazael smothers Ben-hadad with a wet cloth and becomes king in his place -- assassination by suffocation, fulfilling Elisha's prophecy and Elijah's original commission.

annotations.word: took a thick cloth, dipped it in water, and spread it on his face

crossReferences.connection: God's commission to Elijah to anoint Hazael as king of Syria, now fulfilled through Elisha

verses.text: In the fifth year of Joram the son of Ahab king of Israel, Jehoshaphat being king of Judah then, Jehoram the son of Jehoshaphat king of Judah began to reign.
verses.explainer: Jehoram son of Jehoshaphat becomes king of Judah during Joram of Israel's fifth year -- the narrative shifts to Judah to track the southern kingdom's decline.

verses.text: He was thirty-two years old when he began to reign. He reigned eight years in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Jehoram is thirty-two when he becomes king and reigns eight years in Jerusalem -- a relatively short reign suggesting divine displeasure.

verses.text: He walked in the way of the kings of Israel, as did Ahab’s house; for he married Ahab’s daughter. He did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight.
verses.explainer: He walks in the ways of Israel's kings, like the house of Ahab, because he married Ahab's daughter -- the marriage alliance that was supposed to bring political benefits brings spiritual corruption instead.

crossReferences.connection: The prohibition against intermarriage with idolaters, specifically warning that foreign wives would turn hearts away from God

thenVsNow.then: The corruption of Judah through a single marriage alliance illustrates how quickly spiritual compromise can spread through institutional connections.
thenVsNow.now: The corruption of Judah through a single marriage alliance illustrates how quickly spiritual compromise can spread through institutional connections.

verses.text: However Yahweh would not destroy Judah, for David his servant’s sake, as he promised him to give to him a lamp for his children always.
verses.explainer: Yet the LORD does not destroy Judah for David's sake, because He promised David a lamp forever -- the Davidic covenant preserves Judah despite its kings' failures.

crossReferences.connection: The Davidic covenant that guarantees the dynasty's continuation despite individual kings' unfaithfulness

verses.text: In his days Edom revolted from under the hand of Judah, and made a king over themselves.
verses.explainer: Edom revolts against Judah and establishes its own king -- the loss of vassal territories indicates Judah's weakening under Jehoram's leadership.

verses.text: Then Joram passed over to Zair, and all his chariots with him: and he rose up by night, and struck the Edomites who surrounded him, and the captains of the chariots; and the people fled to their tents.
verses.explainer: Jehoram attempts to suppress the Edomite revolt but fails, and his army flees -- military failure accompanies spiritual failure.

verses.text: So Edom revolted from under the hand of Judah to this day. Then Libnah revolted at the same time.
verses.explainer: Edom remains independent, and Libnah also revolts -- multiple territories breaking free simultaneously indicates significant political decline.

verses.text: The rest of the acts of Joram, and all that he did, aren’t they written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?
verses.explainer: The formulaic summary of Jehoram's reign, directing readers to fuller records.

verses.text: Joram slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in David’s city; and Ahaziah his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Jehoram dies and Ahaziah his son reigns -- the Davidic succession continues despite the current dynasty's corruption.

verses.text: In the twelfth year of Joram the son of Ahab king of Israel, Ahaziah the son of Jehoram king of Judah began to reign.
verses.explainer: Ahaziah son of Jehoram becomes king of Judah in Joram of Israel's twelfth year.

verses.text: Ahaziah was twenty-two years old when he began to reign; and he reigned one year in Jerusalem. His mother’s name was Athaliah the daughter of Omri king of Israel.
verses.explainer: Ahaziah is twenty-two years old and reigns only one year; his mother Athaliah is a granddaughter of Omri -- connecting Judah's king directly to the most corrupt dynasty of the northern kingdom.

verses.text: He walked in the way of Ahab’s house, and did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight, as did Ahab’s house; for he was the son-in-law of Ahab’s house.
verses.explainer: Ahaziah walks in the ways of the house of Ahab because he is related to them by marriage -- the corruption flows through family ties, contaminating Judah's worship.

thenVsNow.then: Institutional corruption often spreads through relationship networks and family connections, not just through explicit policy decisions.
thenVsNow.now: Institutional corruption often spreads through relationship networks and family connections, not just through explicit policy decisions.

verses.text: He went with Joram the son of Ahab to war against Hazael king of Syria at Ramoth Gilead, and the Syrians wounded Joram.
verses.explainer: Ahaziah joins Joram of Israel in war against Hazael of Syria at Ramoth-gilead, and Joram is wounded -- the same battlefield where Ahab died, now drawing Judah's king into danger.

crossReferences.connection: Ahab's death at the same Ramoth-gilead battlefield, showing the recurring pattern

verses.text: King Joram returned to be healed in Jezreel from the wounds which the Syrians had given him at Ramah, when he fought against Hazael king of Syria. Ahaziah the son of Jehoram king of Judah went down to see Joram the son of Ahab in Jezreel, because he was sick.
verses.explainer: Joram returns to Jezreel to recover from his wounds, and Ahaziah of Judah goes to visit him -- this visit to Jezreel places Ahaziah at the scene when Jehu's revolution erupts, sealing his fate.

explainer.summary: Elisha sends a young prophet to anoint Jehu as king with the commission to destroy the house of Ahab. Jehu drives furiously to Jezreel, kills King Joram of Israel with an arrow in Naboth's field (fulfilling Elijah's prophecy), then kills King Ahaziah of Judah. He rides into Jezreel where Jezebel, painted and defiant, is thrown from a window and eaten by dogs -- fulfilling Elijah's prophecy to the letter.
themes: The fulfillment of prophetic judgment on Ahab's house
Jehu as instrument of divine wrath
The justice of Naboth's vineyard vindicated
Jezebel's defiant end
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 9 is the dramatic fulfillment of everything Elijah pronounced against Ahab's house. Every prophecy from 1 Kings 21 comes to pass with violent precision. Jehu is God's instrument of judgment, though his own motivations are mixed.

wordStudies.meaning: Manner of driving or conducting. Jehu's characteristic furious chariot driving was so well-known that the watchman could identify him at a distance by his driving style. 'He drives furiously' captures Jehu's personality: intense, reckless, and unstoppable.

Eye paint or cosmetic pigment. Jezebel's painting her face before meeting Jehu was not seduction but royal dignity -- she intended to die as a queen, maintaining her composure and authority to the end.

verses.text: Elisha the prophet called one of the sons of the prophets, and said to him, “Put your belt on your waist, take this vial of oil in your hand, and go to Ramoth Gilead.
verses.explainer: Elisha sends a young prophet to Ramoth-gilead with a flask of oil to anoint Jehu -- the commission Elijah received at Horeb is now being fulfilled through Elisha and his apprentice.

crossReferences.connection: God's original commission to Elijah to anoint Jehu as king of Israel

verses.text: When you come there, find Jehu the son of Jehoshaphat the son of Nimshi, and go in, and make him rise up from among his brothers, and take him to an inner room.
verses.explainer: The young prophet is to find Jehu son of Jehoshaphat son of Nimshi among the military commanders and take him into a private room -- secrecy is essential because this is a revolutionary act.

verses.text: Then take the vial of oil, and pour it on his head, and say, ‘Yahweh says, “I have anointed you king over Israel.”’ Then open the door, flee, and don’t wait.”
verses.explainer: The prophet is to pour oil on Jehu's head, declare 'Thus says the LORD: I have anointed you king over Israel,' then flee immediately -- the anointing triggers a chain of events that cannot be reversed.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Anointing with oil was the Israelite ritual of royal appointment, symbolizing God's Spirit empowering the new king. The instruction to flee immediately shows the danger of being associated with revolutionary acts.

verses.text: So the young man, even the young man, the prophet, went to Ramoth Gilead.
verses.explainer: The young prophet goes to Ramoth-gilead and finds the military commanders sitting together -- the army leadership gathered, about to receive the spark that ignites revolution.

verses.text:
When he came, behold, the captains of the army were sitting. Then he said, “I have a message for you, captain.”
Jehu said, “To which of us all?”
He said, “To you, O captain.”
verses.explainer: He identifies Jehu as the one he has a message for, and Jehu asks 'For which of us?' -- Jehu does not yet know he has been singled out for kingship.

verses.text: He arose, and went into the house. Then he poured the oil on his head, and said to him, “Yahweh, the God of Israel, says, ‘I have anointed you king over the people of Yahweh, even over Israel.
verses.explainer: In private, the prophet anoints Jehu and declares him king over the LORD's people Israel -- the anointing formula emphasizes that Israel belongs to God, not to any human king.

verses.text: You must strike your master Ahab’s house, that I may avenge the blood of my servants the prophets, and the blood of all the servants of Yahweh, at the hand of Jezebel.
verses.explainer: Jehu's commission is specific: strike down the house of Ahab to avenge the blood of God's prophets murdered by Jezebel -- divine justice for the prophets' blood requires the destruction of the entire house.

crossReferences.connection: Jezebel's murder of God's prophets, now being avenged through Jehu's commission

verses.text: For the whole house of Ahab will perish. I will cut off from Ahab everyone who urinates against a wall, both him who is shut up and him who is left at large in Israel.
verses.explainer: The entire house of Ahab will perish, using the same judgment formula pronounced against Jeroboam and Baasha -- the pattern of total dynastic destruction continues.

crossReferences.connection: Elijah's original pronouncement of this judgment against Ahab's house

verses.text: I will make Ahab’s house like the house of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, and like the house of Baasha the son of Ahijah.
verses.explainer: Ahab's house will be like Jeroboam's and Baasha's -- the third dynasty to face identical divine judgment, showing that the pattern of sin produces the pattern of judgment.

verses.text: The dogs will eat Jezebel on the plot of ground of Jezreel, and there shall be no one to bury her.’” Then he opened the door and fled.
verses.explainer: Dogs will eat Jezebel in Jezreel with no burial -- the specific prophecy about Jezebel's end, which will be fulfilled with gruesome precision.

crossReferences.connection: Elijah's original prophecy that dogs would devour Jezebel by the wall of Jezreel

verses.text:
When Jehu came out to the servants of his lord, and one said to him, “Is all well? Why did this mad fellow come to you?”
He said to them, “You know the man and how he talks.”
verses.explainer: Jehu returns to his fellow officers, who ask what the 'madman' wanted -- the soldiers' casual dismissal of the prophet as 'mad' reveals how the military viewed prophetic figures.

verses.text:
They said, “That is a lie. Tell us now.”
He said, “He said to me, ‘Yahweh says, I have anointed you king over Israel.’”
verses.explainer: When pressed, Jehu reveals the anointing and the message -- the secret is out, and the military commanders must decide their allegiance.

verses.text: Then they hurried, and each man took his cloak, and put it under him on the top of the stairs, and blew the trumpet, saying, “Jehu is king.”
verses.explainer: The officers immediately spread their cloaks on the steps and blow a trumpet proclaiming 'Jehu is king!' -- instant military coup, with the army declaring allegiance to their anointed commander.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The military's instant acceptance suggests widespread dissatisfaction with the Omride dynasty. Jehu did not need to persuade his officers -- they were already waiting for a leader willing to act.

verses.text: So Jehu the son of Jehoshaphat the son of Nimshi conspired against Joram. (Now Joram was keeping Ramoth Gilead, he and all Israel, because of Hazael king of Syria;
verses.explainer: Jehu conspires against Joram while Joram is recovering from his wounds at Jezreel -- the wounded king is at his most vulnerable, separated from his army.

thenVsNow.then: Political transitions often occur when leaders are vulnerable -- physically weakened, politically isolated, or distracted by personal crises.
thenVsNow.now: Political transitions often occur when leaders are vulnerable -- physically weakened, politically isolated, or distracted by personal crises.

verses.text: but king Joram had returned to be healed in Jezreel of the wounds which the Syrians had given him, when he fought with Hazael king of Syria.) Jehu said, “If this is your thinking, then let no one escape and go out of the city, to go to tell it in Jezreel.”
verses.explainer: Jehu tells his men to let no one escape to warn Jezreel -- military efficiency in containing information until the strike force arrives.

verses.text: So Jehu rode in a chariot and went to Jezreel, for Joram lay there. Ahaziah king of Judah had come down to see Joram.
verses.explainer: Jehu rides to Jezreel where Joram lies wounded and Ahaziah of Judah is visiting -- both target kings are in the same location, making the revolution devastatingly efficient.

verses.text:
Now the watchman was standing on the tower in Jezreel, and he spied the company of Jehu as he came, and said, “I see a company.”
Joram said, “Take a horseman, and send to meet them, and let him say, ‘Is it peace?’”
verses.explainer: The watchman on Jezreel's tower sees Jehu's company approaching and reports it to Joram -- the unfolding coup viewed from the doomed king's perspective.

verses.text:
So one went on horseback to meet him, and said, “Thus says the king, ‘Is it peace?’”
Jehu said, “What do you have to do with peace? Fall in behind me!”
The watchman said, “The messenger came to them, but he isn’t coming back.”
verses.explainer: Joram sends a horseman asking 'Is it peace?' and Jehu commands him to fall in behind -- the first messenger is absorbed into Jehu's force rather than allowed to return.

verses.text:
Then he sent out a second on horseback, who came to them, and said, “Thus says the king, ‘Is it peace?’”
Jehu answered, “What do you have to do with peace? Fall in behind me!”
verses.explainer: A second messenger is sent with the same question and the same result -- Joram's growing unease as his messengers fail to return.

verses.text: The watchman said, “He came to them, and isn’t coming back. The driving is like the driving of Jehu the son of Nimshi, for he drives furiously.”
verses.explainer: The watchman reports the driving is like Jehu's -- 'he drives furiously' -- and Joram realizes who is coming.

verses.text:
Joram said, “Get ready!”
They got his chariot ready. Then Joram king of Israel and Ahaziah king of Judah went out, each in his chariot, and they went out to meet Jehu, and found him on Naboth the Jezreelite’s land.
verses.explainer: Joram and Ahaziah each ride out in their chariots and meet Jehu at the property of Naboth the Jezreelite -- the confrontation occurs at the very spot of Ahab's greatest crime.

crossReferences.connection: Elijah's prophecy that judgment would come at Naboth's vineyard, now fulfilled

verses.text:
When Joram saw Jehu, he said, “Is it peace, Jehu?”
He answered, “What peace, so long as the prostitution of your mother Jezebel and her witchcraft abound?”
verses.explainer: Joram asks 'Is it peace?' and Jehu replies 'What peace, as long as the harlotries of your mother Jezebel and her sorceries are so many?' -- the revolution is theological, not merely political.

verses.text: Joram turned his hands, and fled, and said to Ahaziah, “This is treason, Ahaziah!”
verses.explainer: Joram turns to flee, crying to Ahaziah 'Treachery!' -- too late recognizing the danger and attempting to warn his ally.

thenVsNow.then: The cry of 'Treachery!' from those being judged is common when consequences finally arrive -- but the real treachery was Ahab's house against God's covenant.
thenVsNow.now: The cry of 'Treachery!' from those being judged is common when consequences finally arrive -- but the real treachery was Ahab's house against God's covenant.

verses.text: Jehu drew his bow with his full strength, and struck Joram between his arms; and the arrow went out at his heart, and he sunk down in his chariot.
verses.explainer: Jehu draws his bow and strikes Joram between the shoulders, the arrow piercing his heart, and he collapses in his chariot -- the king of Israel is killed by an arrow, just as his father Ahab was.

crossReferences.connection: Ahab was also killed by an arrow in battle -- father and son meet the same fate by the same weapon

verses.text: Then Jehu said to Bidkar his captain, “Pick him up, and throw him in the plot of the field of Naboth the Jezreelite; for remember how, when you and I rode together after Ahab his father, Yahweh laid this burden on him:
verses.explainer: Jehu tells his officer to throw Joram's body on Naboth's field, recalling how he and the officer once heard Elijah's prophecy against Ahab at this very spot -- the personal memory of prophetic words being fulfilled.

verses.text: ‘Surely I have seen yesterday the blood of Naboth, and the blood of his sons,’ says Yahweh; ‘and I will repay you in this plot of ground,’ says Yahweh. Now therefore take and cast him onto the plot of ground, according to Yahweh’s word.”
verses.explainer: Jehu invokes God's word: 'I will repay you on this plot of ground' -- the vineyard stolen through judicial murder becomes the graveyard of the stealing dynasty.

verses.text: But when Ahaziah the king of Judah saw this, he fled by the way of the garden house. Jehu followed after him, and said, “Strike him also in the chariot!” They struck him at the ascent of Gur, which is by Ibleam. He fled to Megiddo, and died there.
verses.explainer: Ahaziah of Judah flees but Jehu pursues and wounds him near Ibleam; Ahaziah escapes to Megiddo where he dies -- Judah's king, corrupted by alliance with Ahab's house, shares their fate.

verses.text: His servants carried him in a chariot to Jerusalem, and buried him in his tomb with his fathers in David’s city.
verses.explainer: Ahaziah's servants bring his body to Jerusalem and bury him in the city of David -- he receives royal burial in Jerusalem, unlike the northern kings.

verses.text: In the eleventh year of Joram the son of Ahab, Ahaziah began to reign over Judah.
verses.explainer: A chronological note about Ahaziah's accession -- the narrator maintains the framework of synchronized dating between the two kingdoms.

verses.text: When Jehu had come to Jezreel, Jezebel heard of it; and she painted her eyes, and adorned her head, and looked out at the window.
verses.explainer: Jezebel hears Jehu is coming, paints her eyes, adorns her hair, and looks out the window -- not seduction but royal defiance, determined to die as a queen.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Eye paint (kohl) and elaborate hair were markers of royal status, not necessarily sexuality. Jezebel's preparations show she intended to face death with the same fierce pride that characterized her entire life.

verses.text: As Jehu entered in at the gate, she said, “Do you come in peace, Zimri, you murderer of your master?”
verses.explainer: She calls out 'Is it peace, Zimri, you murderer of your master?' -- comparing Jehu to Zimri, who murdered his way to the throne and reigned only seven days, suggesting Jehu will meet a similar end.

crossReferences.connection: Zimri's seven-day reign that Jezebel invokes as a curse on Jehu

verses.text:
He lifted up his face to the window, and said, “Who is on my side? Who?”
Two or three eunuchs looked out at him.
verses.explainer: Jehu looks up and calls 'Who is on my side?' and two or three eunuchs look out the window -- even Jezebel's own household servants are willing to betray her.

verses.text:
He said, “Throw her down!”
So they threw her down; and some of her blood was sprinkled on the wall, and on the horses. Then he trampled her under foot.
verses.explainer: They throw Jezebel from the window, her blood spatters the wall, and horses trample her body -- the violent end of the woman who engineered Naboth's murder and promoted Baal worship throughout Israel.

thenVsNow.then: Jezebel's end fulfills the principle that those who use power to destroy others eventually have power turned against them -- the violence she inflicted comes back upon her.
thenVsNow.now: Jezebel's end fulfills the principle that those who use power to destroy others eventually have power turned against them -- the violence she inflicted comes back upon her.

verses.text: When he had come in, he ate and drank. Then he said, “See now to this cursed woman, and bury her; for she is a king’s daughter.”
verses.explainer: Jehu goes in to eat and drink, then says 'See to this cursed woman and bury her, for she is a king's daughter' -- even Jehu acknowledges Jezebel's royal status and orders burial.

verses.text: They went to bury her, but they found no more of her than the skull, the feet, and the palms of her hands.
verses.explainer: When they go to bury her, they find only skull, feet, and palms -- dogs have consumed the rest, exactly as Elijah prophesied.

crossReferences.connection: Elijah's prophecy that dogs would devour Jezebel by the wall of Jezreel, fulfilled to the letter

verses.text:
Therefore they came back, and told him.
He said, “This is Yahweh’s word, which he spoke by his servant Elijah the Tishbite, saying, ‘The dogs will eat the flesh of Jezebel on the plot of Jezreel,
verses.explainer: They report to Jehu, who recognizes the fulfillment of Elijah's prophecy about Jezebel's body being eaten by dogs in Jezreel -- the prophetic word, spoken years earlier, is fulfilled with precise detail.

verses.text: and the body of Jezebel will be as dung on the face of the field on Jezreel’s land, so that they won’t say, “This is Jezebel.”’”
verses.explainer: Jezebel's remains will be like dung on the surface of the field, so no one can say 'This is Jezebel's grave' -- denied even the dignity of an identifiable burial site.

thenVsNow.then: The complete erasure of Jezebel's burial -- no grave, no monument, no memorial -- stands as the ultimate reversal for someone who wielded enormous power and influence.
thenVsNow.now: The complete erasure of Jezebel's burial -- no grave, no monument, no memorial -- stands as the ultimate reversal for someone who wielded enormous power and influence.

explainer.summary: Jehu systematically destroys every remnant of Ahab's house: seventy sons beheaded in Samaria, relatives of Ahaziah of Judah killed, and finally all Baal worshippers lured into a temple and massacred. Jehu destroys the Baal temple and pillar, ending organized Baal worship in Israel. Yet he maintains the golden calf worship at Dan and Bethel, earning a qualified divine commendation but incomplete reform.
themes: The total destruction of Ahab's dynasty
Purging Baal worship from Israel
The limits of political revolution without spiritual transformation
Violence as divine instrument and its moral complexity
explainer.contextInBook: Jehu's revolution fulfills every prophecy against Ahab's house but falls short of true spiritual reform. By maintaining Jeroboam's golden calves, Jehu shows that political revolution without genuine covenant commitment merely replaces one form of apostasy with another.

wordStudies.meaning: Zeal, jealousy, or passionate devotion. Jehu invites Jehonadab to 'come and see my zeal for the LORD,' but his zeal is selective -- destroying Baal while maintaining Jeroboam's calves. True zeal would address all idolatry, not just politically convenient targets.

Subtlety or cunning. Jehu uses deception to gather all Baal worshippers into one place for destruction. The narrator acknowledges the strategic brilliance while the theological verdict is mixed -- effective but morally complex.

verses.text: Now Ahab had seventy sons in Samaria. Jehu wrote letters, and sent to Samaria, to the rulers of Jezreel, even the elders, and to those who brought up Ahab’s sons, saying,
verses.explainer: Ahab has seventy sons in Samaria. Jehu sends letters to the rulers of Samaria and the guardians of Ahab's sons, challenging them to fight for their master's house -- testing whether anyone will resist.

verses.text: “Now as soon as this letter comes to you, since your master’s sons are with you, and you have chariots and horses, a fortified city also, and armor,
verses.explainer: The letter demands they choose the best of their master's sons and fight for their master's house -- a challenge that tests the loyalty and courage of Ahab's remaining supporters.

verses.text: Select the best and fittest of your master’s sons, set him on his father’s throne, and fight for your master’s house.”
verses.explainer: Jehu tells them to put their chosen prince on the throne and fight -- forcing a decisive confrontation between the old dynasty and the new.

verses.text: But they were exceedingly afraid, and said, “Behold, the two kings didn’t stand before him! How then shall we stand?”
verses.explainer: The terrified officials reason that two kings could not stand against Jehu, so they certainly cannot -- fear overcomes loyalty, and Ahab's supporters capitulate without resistance.

thenVsNow.then: When people realize which way the wind is blowing, self-preservation usually overcomes loyalty to the fallen regime.
thenVsNow.now: When people realize which way the wind is blowing, self-preservation usually overcomes loyalty to the fallen regime.

verses.text: He who was over the household, and he who was over the city, the elders also, and those who raised the children, sent to Jehu, saying, “We are your servants, and will do all that you ask us. We will not make any man king. You do that which is good in your eyes.”
verses.explainer: The officials surrender completely, saying 'We are your servants and will do whatever you tell us' -- total capitulation that places Ahab's sons at Jehu's mercy.

verses.text:
Then he wrote a letter the second time to them, saying, “If you are on my side, and if you will listen to my voice, take the heads of the men who are your master’s sons, and come to me to Jezreel by tomorrow this time.”
Now the king’s sons, being seventy persons, were with the great men of the city, who brought them up.
verses.explainer: Jehu writes a second letter: if you are on my side, bring the heads of your master's sons to Jezreel by tomorrow -- forcing the officials to make themselves complicit in the dynasty's destruction.

verses.text: When the letter came to them, they took the king’s sons and killed them, even seventy people, and put their heads in baskets, and sent them to him to Jezreel.
verses.explainer: They kill all seventy sons, put their heads in baskets, and send them to Jezreel -- the officials become instruments of destruction, implicating themselves and eliminating any possibility of future resistance.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Sending decapitated heads was a common proof of execution in the ancient Near East. The Assyrians routinely displayed enemy heads. The seventy heads demonstrate the total elimination of Ahab's male line.

verses.text:
A messenger came and told him, “They have brought the heads of the king’s sons.”
He said, “Lay them in two heaps at the entrance of the gate until the morning.”
verses.explainer: When told the heads have arrived, Jehu commands them piled in two heaps at the city gate until morning -- a public display designed to demonstrate the dynasty's complete destruction.

annotations.word: two heaps at the entrance of the gate

verses.text: In the morning, he went out, and stood, and said to all the people, “You are righteous. Behold, I conspired against my master and killed him, but who killed all these?
verses.explainer: In the morning Jehu addresses the people, claiming he only killed one king while others killed these seventy -- deflecting responsibility while making the point that the judgment was inevitable.

annotations.word: I conspired against my master and killed him, but who killed all these?

verses.text: Know now that nothing will fall to the earth of Yahweh’s word, which Yahweh spoke concerning Ahab’s house. For Yahweh has done that which he spoke by his servant Elijah.”
verses.explainer: Jehu interprets the events as fulfillment of God's word through Elijah against Ahab's house -- theological justification for political violence.

crossReferences.connection: Elijah's prophecy of total destruction for Ahab's house, now being fulfilled by Jehu

verses.text: So Jehu struck all that remained of Ahab’s house in Jezreel, with all his great men, his familiar friends, and his priests, until he left him no one remaining.
verses.explainer: Jehu kills all who remain of Ahab's house in Jezreel -- all his great men, friends, and priests -- leaving no survivors.

verses.text: He arose and departed, and went to Samaria. As he was at the shearing house of the shepherds on the way,
verses.explainer: Jehu encounters relatives of Ahaziah of Judah traveling to visit the royal family -- they have not heard about the revolution and walk into danger.

thenVsNow.then: These Judahite princes' ignorance of the revolution illustrates how rapidly political situations can change, catching those who are disconnected from current events.
thenVsNow.now: These Judahite princes' ignorance of the revolution illustrates how rapidly political situations can change, catching those who are disconnected from current events.

verses.text:
Jehu met with the brothers of Ahaziah king of Judah, and said, “Who are you?”
They answered, “We are the brothers of Ahaziah. We are going down to greet the children of the king and the children of the queen.”
verses.explainer: Jehu identifies forty-two relatives of Ahaziah and orders them all killed at the cistern of Beth-eked -- Judah's royal house is also punished for its alliance with Ahab's family.

verses.text:
He said, “Take them alive!”
They took them alive, and killed them at the pit of the shearing house, even forty-two men. He didn’t leave any of them.
verses.explainer: They kill all forty-two at the cistern, leaving none alive -- the judgment on Ahab's house extends to Judah's royals who were connected by marriage.

verses.text:
When he had departed from there, he met Jehonadab the son of Rechab coming to meet him. He greeted him, and said to him, “Is your heart right, as my heart is with your heart?”
Jehonadab answered, “It is.”
“If it is, give me your hand.” He gave him his hand; and he took him up to him into the chariot.
verses.explainer: Jehu meets Jehonadab son of Rechab and invites him to witness his 'zeal for the LORD' -- enlisting a known religious purist as a witness to legitimize the continuing purge.

crossReferences.connection: The Rechabites' continuing faithfulness to their ancestor Jehonadab's commands, centuries later

verses.text: He said, “Come with me, and see my zeal for Yahweh.” So they made him ride in his chariot.
verses.explainer: Jehu invites Jehonadab: 'Come with me and see my zeal for the LORD' -- impressive words, but Jehu's zeal is selective, as the narrator will soon reveal.

verses.text: When he came to Samaria, he struck all who remained to Ahab in Samaria, until he had destroyed him, according to Yahweh’s word, which he spoke to Elijah.
verses.explainer: Jehu kills every remaining member of Ahab's household in Samaria, fulfilling God's word to Elijah -- the destruction is total and systematic.

verses.text: Jehu gathered all the people together, and said to them, “Ahab served Baal a little; but Jehu will serve him much.
verses.explainer: Jehu announces he will serve Baal even more than Ahab did and summons all Baal's prophets, servants, and priests -- a deception to gather all Baal worshippers into one place.

verses.text: Now therefore call to me all the prophets of Baal, all of his worshipers, and all of his priests. Let no one be absent; for I have a great sacrifice to Baal. Whoever is absent, he shall not live.” But Jehu did deceptively, intending to destroy the worshipers of Baal.
verses.explainer: Jehu issues a solemn assembly for Baal, warning that anyone absent will die -- ensuring complete attendance at what will become a mass execution.

verses.text:
Jehu said, “Sanctify a solemn assembly for Baal!”
So they proclaimed it.
verses.explainer: Jehu proclaims a solemn assembly for Baal -- using the language of legitimate worship to set the trap.

verses.text: Jehu sent through all Israel; and all the worshipers of Baal came, so that there was not a man left that didn’t come. They came into the house of Baal; and the house of Baal was filled from one end to another.
verses.explainer: Every Baal worshipper comes, filling the temple of Baal from one end to the other -- the trap works perfectly, gathering the entire Baal-worshipping community in one building.

verses.text:
He said to him who kept the wardrobe, “Bring out robes for all the worshipers of Baal!”
So he brought robes out to them.
verses.explainer: Jehu provides vestments for all the Baal worshippers -- identifying garments that also serve to distinguish them from any Yahweh worshippers who might be present.

verses.text: Jehu went with Jehonadab the son of Rechab into the house of Baal. Then he said to the worshipers of Baal, “Search, and see that none of the servants of Yahweh are here with you, but only the worshipers of Baal.”
verses.explainer: Jehu and Jehonadab enter the temple and verify that no servant of the LORD is present -- a final check before the execution, ensuring only Baal worshippers are targeted.

verses.text: So they went in to offer sacrifices and burnt offerings. Now Jehu had appointed for himself eighty men outside, and said, “If any of the men whom I bring into your hands escape, he who lets him go, his life shall be for the life of him.”
verses.explainer: While they offer sacrifices, Jehu stations eighty guards outside with orders to kill everyone inside -- the worship service becomes a killing ground.

verses.text: As soon as he had finished offering the burnt offering, Jehu said to the guard and to the captains, “Go in and kill them! Let no one escape.” So they struck them with the edge of the sword. The guard and the captains threw the bodies out, and went to the inner shrine of the house of Baal.
verses.explainer: After the burnt offering, Jehu commands the guards to go in and kill everyone -- the mass execution of Baal's entire priesthood and worship community in Israel.

verses.text: They brought out the pillars that were in the house of Baal, and burned them.
verses.explainer: They bring out the sacred pillar of Baal and burn it -- destroying the central cult object of Baal worship in Israel.

verses.text: They broke down the pillar of Baal, and broke down the house of Baal, and made it a latrine, to this day.
verses.explainer: They demolish the pillar and temple of Baal and turn it into a latrine -- the ultimate desecration, transforming the holy site of Baal worship into a place of human waste.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Turning a temple into a latrine was deliberately insulting, ensuring permanent defilement of the site. Archaeological parallels exist where conquest involved deliberate defilement of sacred spaces.

verses.text: Thus Jehu destroyed Baal out of Israel.
