verses.explainer: Jehu destroys Baal out of Israel -- a sweeping statement that organized Baal worship is eliminated from the northern kingdom.

thenVsNow.then: Political revolution can eliminate external institutions of false worship, but it cannot transform hearts -- as the very next verse demonstrates.
thenVsNow.now: Political revolution can eliminate external institutions of false worship, but it cannot transform hearts -- as the very next verse demonstrates.

verses.text: However, Jehu didn’t depart from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, with which he made Israel to sin, the golden calves that were in Bethel and that were in Dan.
verses.explainer: However, Jehu does not depart from the sins of Jeroboam -- the golden calves at Dan and Bethel remain, exposing the limits of Jehu's reform.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Jehu, “Because you have done well in executing that which is right in my eyes, and have done to Ahab’s house according to all that was in my heart, your descendants shall sit on the throne of Israel to the fourth generation.”
verses.explainer: God commends Jehu for carrying out what was right regarding Ahab's house and promises four generations on the throne -- a qualified reward for an incomplete obedience.

verses.text: But Jehu took no heed to walk in the law of Yahweh, the God of Israel, with all his heart. He didn’t depart from the sins of Jeroboam, with which he made Israel to sin.
verses.explainer: But Jehu is not careful to walk in the law of God with all his heart, continuing Jeroboam's sins -- the theological verdict is mixed: commendation for destroying Baal, condemnation for keeping the calves.

thenVsNow.then: Partial obedience -- doing some of what God requires while ignoring the rest -- is a pattern that limits spiritual growth in every era. Selective reform is still incomplete reform.
thenVsNow.now: Partial obedience -- doing some of what God requires while ignoring the rest -- is a pattern that limits spiritual growth in every era. Selective reform is still incomplete reform.

verses.text: In those days Yahweh began to cut away parts of Israel; and Hazael struck them in all the borders of Israel;
verses.explainer: In those days the LORD begins to reduce Israel's territory through Hazael of Syria -- the consequences of incomplete faithfulness begin immediately.

crossReferences.connection: Elisha's prophecy of the atrocities Hazael would commit against Israel, now beginning to unfold

verses.text: from the Jordan eastward, all the land of Gilead, the Gadites, and the Reubenites, and the Manassites, from Aroer, which is by the valley of the Arnon, even Gilead and Bashan.
verses.explainer: Hazael conquers all of Israel's territory east of the Jordan -- Gilead, Gad, Reuben, and Manasseh -- the Transjordanian territories are lost to Syria.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Jehu, and all that he did, and all his might, aren’t they written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel?
verses.explainer: The formulaic summary of Jehu's reign and military achievements.

verses.text: Jehu slept with his fathers; and they buried him in Samaria. Jehoahaz his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Jehu dies and his son Jehoahaz succeeds him -- the dynasty begins its four-generation run on Israel's throne.

verses.text: The time that Jehu reigned over Israel in Samaria was twenty-eight years.
verses.explainer: Jehu reigned twenty-eight years -- a substantial reign reflecting God's reward for destroying Baal worship, even though his faithfulness was incomplete.

explainer.summary: When Athaliah, Ahab's daughter, learns her son Ahaziah is dead, she seizes Judah's throne and kills all the royal family -- nearly exterminating the Davidic line. But Jehosheba hides the infant Joash in the temple for six years. Jehoiada the priest orchestrates a coup, crowns Joash, executes Athaliah, and renews the covenant. The Davidic line survives by a single child.
themes: The near-extinction and miraculous preservation of David's line
The priest as guardian of the covenant
The danger of foreign religious influence through marriage
Covenant renewal after apostasy
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter represents the most dangerous moment for the Davidic covenant. If Athaliah had succeeded in killing all of David's descendants, God's promise to David would have failed. The preservation of one infant becomes the hinge on which all of redemptive history turns.

wordStudies.meaning: A binding agreement or treaty. Jehoiada makes a three-way covenant: between God and the king, between God and the people, and between the king and the people. This renewal restores the covenant framework that Athaliah had destroyed.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The written law or covenant document
wordStudies.meaning: The written law or covenant document. Placing the 'testimony' on young Joash at his coronation symbolized that the king was subject to God's law -- kingship in Judah was constitutional, limited by divine covenant.

verses.text: Now when Athaliah the mother of Ahaziah saw that her son was dead, she arose and destroyed all the royal offspring.
verses.explainer: When Athaliah sees her son is dead, she destroys all the royal family -- the daughter of Ahab and Jezebel attempts to exterminate the Davidic dynasty entirely.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Athaliah's attempted genocide of the Davidic line represents the closest the covenant promise to David ever came to failure. Only God's providential preservation of one infant prevented the total extinction of the messianic line.

crossReferences.connection: God's promise of an eternal Davidic dynasty, which Athaliah nearly destroys

verses.text: But Jehosheba, the daughter of king Joram, sister of Ahaziah, took Joash the son of Ahaziah, and stole him away from among the king’s sons who were slain, even him and his nurse, and put them in the bedroom; and they hid him from Athaliah, so that he was not slain.
verses.explainer: Jehosheba, Joram's daughter and Ahaziah's sister, steals the infant Joash from among the princes being murdered and hides him with his nurse in a bedroom -- one woman's courage saves the Davidic line.

thenVsNow.then: The preservation of an entire covenant promise depends on one woman's act of courage. Individual faithfulness can have consequences far beyond what anyone imagines at the time.
thenVsNow.now: The preservation of an entire covenant promise depends on one woman's act of courage. Individual faithfulness can have consequences far beyond what anyone imagines at the time.

verses.text: He was with her hidden in Yahweh’s house six years while Athaliah reigned over the land.
verses.explainer: Joash is hidden in the temple for six years while Athaliah rules -- the temple of the LORD becomes the sanctuary that protects the LORD's anointed, hidden from the usurper.

verses.text: In the seventh year Jehoiada sent and fetched the captains over hundreds of the Carites and of the guard, and brought them to him into Yahweh’s house; and he made a covenant with them, and made a covenant with them in Yahweh’s house, and showed them the king’s son.
verses.explainer: In the seventh year, Jehoiada the priest organizes a coup with the palace guard commanders, showing them the young king -- the priest becomes the guardian of the dynasty when the monarchy itself has failed.

verses.text: He commanded them, saying, “This is what you must do: a third of you, who come in on the Sabbath, shall be keepers of the watch of the king’s house;
verses.explainer: Jehoiada organizes the guard into three companies with specific assignments for the coup -- meticulous military planning by a priest, not a general.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The coup is organized during the changing of the guard on the Sabbath, when both incoming and outgoing shifts would be present, doubling the available loyal troops.

verses.text: a third of you shall be at the gate Sur; and a third of you at the gate behind the guard. So you shall keep the watch of the house, and be a barrier.
verses.explainer: The third of the guard at the gate Sur and the third at the gate behind the guards -- strategic placement to control all access points to the temple complex.

verses.text: The two companies of you, even all who go out on the Sabbath, shall keep the watch of Yahweh’s house around the king.
verses.explainer: Two companies guard the temple on the Sabbath and protect the king -- every angle is covered to ensure the young king's safety during the vulnerable coronation moment.

verses.text: You shall surround the king, every man with his weapons in his hand; and he who comes within the ranks, let him be slain. Be with the king when he goes out, and when he comes in.”
verses.explainer: Anyone who approaches the ranks is to be killed; the guards must surround the king at all times -- maximum security for the most vulnerable moment of the restoration.

verses.text: The captains over hundreds did according to all that Jehoiada the priest commanded; and they each took his men, those who were to come in on the Sabbath, with those who were to go out on the Sabbath, and came to Jehoiada the priest.
verses.explainer: The commanders follow Jehoiada's instructions exactly -- military discipline under priestly leadership, an unusual but effective combination.

verses.text: The priest delivered to the captains over hundreds the spears and shields that had been king David’s, which were in Yahweh’s house.
verses.explainer: Jehoiada distributes David's own spears and shields from the temple treasury -- arming the guards with the original weapons of Israel's greatest king adds symbolic weight to the restoration of David's line.

verses.text: The guard stood, every man with his weapons in his hand, from the right side of the house to the left side of the house, along by the altar and the house, around the king.
verses.explainer: The guards take positions around the temple from south side to north, surrounding the altar and the temple -- the young king is protected by a wall of armed men.

thenVsNow.then: The physical protection of this one child determined the future of the Davidic covenant and, Christians would say, the genealogy of Jesus. The stakes of this moment were cosmic, though they appeared merely political.
thenVsNow.now: The physical protection of this one child determined the future of the Davidic covenant and, Christians would say, the genealogy of Jesus. The stakes of this moment were cosmic, though they appeared merely political.

verses.text: Then he brought out the king’s son, and put the crown on him, and gave him the covenant; and they made him king, and anointed him; and they clapped their hands, and said, “Long live the king!”
verses.explainer: Jehoiada brings out Joash, places the crown and the testimony on him, anoints him, and the people clap and shout 'Long live the king!' -- the Davidic monarchy is restored after six years of usurpation.

crossReferences.connection: The law requiring the king to have a copy of the Torah and govern according to it

verses.text: When Athaliah heard the noise of the guard and of the people, she came to the people into Yahweh’s house:
verses.explainer: Athaliah hears the noise of the guards and the people and comes to the temple -- the sounds of celebration alert the usurper that her reign is ending.

verses.text: and she looked, and behold, the king stood by the pillar, as the tradition was, with the captains and the trumpets by the king; and all the people of the land rejoiced, and blew trumpets. Then Athaliah tore her clothes, and cried, “Treason! Treason!”
verses.explainer: She sees the young king standing by the pillar with trumpets and rejoicing, and tears her clothes crying 'Treason! Treason!' -- the usurper accuses the legitimate restoration of being treasonous.

verses.text: Jehoiada the priest commanded the captains of hundreds who were set over the army, and said to them, “Bring her out between the ranks. Kill anyone who follows her with the sword.” For the priest said, “Don’t let her be slain in Yahweh’s house.”
verses.explainer: Jehoiada orders Athaliah escorted out between the ranks and killed outside the temple, not wanting her blood shed in God's house -- even in executing justice, the sanctity of the temple is preserved.

verses.text: So they made way for her; and she went by the way of the horses’ entry to the king’s house, and she was slain there.
verses.explainer: They seize her and put her to death at the horse entrance to the palace -- executed at the doorway of the power she wrongfully seized.

verses.text: Jehoiada made a covenant between Yahweh and the king and the people, that they should be Yahweh’s people; also between the king and the people.
verses.explainer: Jehoiada makes a covenant between the LORD, the king, and the people that they will be God's people, and another between the king and the people -- a three-way covenant restoration.

thenVsNow.then: Covenant renewal after a period of apostasy is a recurring biblical pattern. When faithfulness has been abandoned, explicit recommitment -- not just resumption of routine -- is needed.
thenVsNow.now: Covenant renewal after a period of apostasy is a recurring biblical pattern. When faithfulness has been abandoned, explicit recommitment -- not just resumption of routine -- is needed.

verses.text: All the people of the land went to the house of Baal, and broke it down; his altars and his images broke they in pieces thoroughly, and killed Mattan the priest of Baal before the altars. The priest appointed officers over Yahweh’s house.
verses.explainer: The people tear down the temple of Baal, smash its altars and images, and kill the priest of Baal -- the purging of Baal worship that Athaliah had introduced into Judah.

crossReferences.connection: Jehu's similar destruction of the Baal temple in Samaria -- both kingdoms purge Baal worship

verses.text: He took the captains over hundreds, and the Carites, and the guard, and all the people of the land; and they brought down the king from Yahweh’s house, and came by the way of the gate of the guard to the king’s house. He sat on the throne of the kings.
verses.explainer: Jehoiada brings the young king from the temple to the palace, and he takes his seat on the royal throne -- the physical transfer from sanctuary to palace symbolizes the completed restoration of legitimate rule.

verses.text: So all the people of the land rejoiced, and the city was quiet. They had slain Athaliah with the sword at the king’s house.
verses.explainer: All the people of the land rejoice, and the city is quiet after Athaliah's death -- peace replaces the terror of her regime.

verses.text: Jehoash was seven years old when he began to reign.
verses.explainer: Joash is seven years old when he becomes king -- the youngest king in Judah's history, relying entirely on Jehoiada's guidance through his childhood.

explainer.summary: Under Jehoiada's guidance, Joash initiates temple repairs that the priests have neglected. When the priests fail to act, Joash creates a direct collection system using a chest at the temple entrance. The repairs proceed successfully, but later Joash bribes Hazael of Syria with temple treasures to avoid an attack on Jerusalem. After Jehoiada's death, Joash's officials assassinate him.
themes: Temple maintenance as spiritual responsibility
Institutional reform of corrupt systems
The danger of depending on a mentor rather than developing personal faith
The cost of political survival
explainer.contextInBook: Joash's reign illustrates the principle that externally directed faith -- faith that depends on another person's influence -- collapses when the supporting person is removed. His later decline after Jehoiada's death is one of the saddest stories in Kings.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A crack, breach, or structural damage in a buildin
wordStudies.meaning: A crack, breach, or structural damage in a building. The temple's disrepair after decades of neglect under Athaliah symbolized the broader spiritual deterioration. Repairing the physical building represented repairing the covenant relationship.

A box or chest. The same word used for the Ark of the Covenant is used for Joash's collection box -- an intentional connection between the most sacred object and the practical means of maintaining God's house.

verses.text: Jehoash began to reign in the seventh year of Jehu, and he reigned forty years in Jerusalem. His mother’s name was Zibiah of Beersheba.
verses.explainer: In Jehu's seventh year, Joash begins to reign, and he reigns forty years in Jerusalem -- a long reign, though its quality declines dramatically after Jehoiada's death.

verses.text: Jehoash did that which was right in Yahweh’s eyes all his days in which Jehoiada the priest instructed him.
verses.explainer: Joash does right all the days Jehoiada the priest instructs him -- the qualification 'all the days Jehoiada instructed him' is ominous, implying that after Jehoiada, things changed.

thenVsNow.then: Faith that depends entirely on another person's influence rather than personal conviction is inherently fragile and cannot survive the loss of that external support.
thenVsNow.now: Faith that depends entirely on another person's influence rather than personal conviction is inherently fragile and cannot survive the loss of that external support.

verses.text: However the high places were not taken away. The people still sacrificed and burned incense in the high places.
verses.explainer: The high places are not removed -- the standard limitation of even the better Judahite kings.

verses.text: Jehoash said to the priests, “All the money of the holy things that is brought into Yahweh’s house, in current money, the money of the people for whom each man is evaluated, and all the money that it comes into any man’s heart to bring into Yahweh’s house,
verses.explainer: Joash tells the priests to use all the money brought to the temple -- assessments, vows, and voluntary gifts -- for temple repairs.

verses.text: let the priests take it to them, each man from his donor; and they shall repair the damage to the house, wherever any damage is found.”
verses.explainer: The priests are to repair whatever breach they find in the temple from their portion of the donations -- a reasonable plan that relies on priestly integrity.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The temple had suffered decades of neglect under Athaliah, who had stripped its treasures for Baal worship (2 Chr 24:7). Structural repairs were urgently needed.

verses.text: But it was so, that in the twenty-third year of king Jehoash the priests had not repaired the damage to the house.
verses.explainer: By the twenty-third year of Joash's reign, the priests still have not repaired the temple -- institutional inertia and priestly self-interest have delayed the work for over two decades.

thenVsNow.then: Institutional reform is painfully slow when those responsible for implementing it benefit from the status quo.
thenVsNow.now: Institutional reform is painfully slow when those responsible for implementing it benefit from the status quo.

verses.text: Then king Jehoash called for Jehoiada the priest, and for the other priests, and said to them, “Why don’t you repair the damage to the house? Now therefore take no more money from your treasurers, but deliver it for repair of the damage to the house.”
verses.explainer: Joash confronts the priests: why have you not repaired the temple? He removes their control over the funds -- direct royal intervention when institutional self-regulation fails.

verses.text: The priests consented that they should take no more money from the people, and not repair the damage to the house.
verses.explainer: The priests agree to no longer take money from the people or be responsible for repairs -- a negotiated resolution that separates fundraising from spending.

verses.text: But Jehoiada the priest took a chest, and bored a hole in its lid, and set it beside the altar, on the right side as one comes into Yahweh’s house; and the priests who kept the threshold put all the money that was brought into Yahweh’s house into it.
verses.explainer: Jehoiada takes a chest, bores a hole in its lid, and places it beside the altar -- creating a transparent, public collection system that bypasses priestly control.

verses.text: When they saw that there was much money in the chest, the king’s scribe and the high priest came up, and they put it in bags and counted the money that was found in Yahweh’s house.
verses.explainer: When the chest is full, the royal secretary and high priest count the money together and give it to the workmen -- accountability through dual oversight, combining royal and priestly authority.

verses.text: They gave the money that was weighed out into the hands of those who did the work, who had the oversight of Yahweh’s house; and they paid it out to the carpenters and the builders, who worked on Yahweh’s house,
verses.explainer: They pay carpenters, builders, masons, and stonecutters, and buy timber and stone -- the practical work of temple restoration proceeds efficiently under the new system.

verses.text: and to the masons and the stone cutters, and for buying timber and cut stone to repair the damage to Yahweh’s house, and for all that was laid out for the house to repair it.
verses.explainer: The money goes to repair the breaches -- focused spending on the intended purpose.

verses.text: But there were not made for Yahweh’s house cups of silver, snuffers, basins, trumpets, any vessels of gold, or vessels of silver, of the money that was brought into Yahweh’s house;
verses.explainer: No silver vessels or gold items are made from the money -- it goes entirely to structural repairs, prioritizing the building's integrity over decorative additions.

verses.text: for they gave that to those who did the work, and repaired Yahweh’s house with it.
verses.explainer: The workmen are so honest that no accounting is required of them -- a remarkable note of trust in an ancient narrative about institutional reform.

verses.text: Moreover they didn’t demand an accounting from the men into whose hand they delivered the money to give to those who did the work; for they dealt faithfully.
verses.explainer: The money from guilt and sin offerings is not put in the collection box but goes to the priests -- a compromise that preserves some priestly income while redirecting other funds to repairs.

verses.text: The money for the trespass offerings, and the money for the sin offerings was not brought into Yahweh’s house. It was the priests’.
verses.explainer: There is no accounting demanded of the workers because they deal honestly -- integrity at the individual level succeeds where institutional control failed.

thenVsNow.then: The workmen's honesty contrasts with the priests' earlier failure to use funds properly. Personal integrity is ultimately more reliable than institutional oversight.
thenVsNow.now: The workmen's honesty contrasts with the priests' earlier failure to use funds properly. Personal integrity is ultimately more reliable than institutional oversight.

verses.text: Then Hazael king of Syria went up, and fought against Gath, and took it; and Hazael set his face to go up to Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Hazael of Syria conquers Gath and turns toward Jerusalem -- the military threat that Elisha foresaw is now directed at Judah.

crossReferences.connection: Elisha's prophecy of Hazael's violence against Israel, now extending to Judah

verses.text: Jehoash king of Judah took all the holy things that Jehoshaphat and Jehoram and Ahaziah, his fathers, kings of Judah, had dedicated, and his own holy things, and all the gold that was found in the treasures of Yahweh’s house, and of the king’s house, and sent it to Hazael king of Syria; and he went away from Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Joash takes all the sacred gifts accumulated by previous kings and from the temple's gold and sends it to Hazael, who withdraws from Jerusalem -- buying peace with temple treasures.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Using temple treasures to pay off invaders was a recurring pattern in Judah's history. The temple functioned as both a worship center and a national treasury, making it the resource of last resort in military crises.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Joash, and all that he did, aren’t they written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?
verses.explainer: The remaining acts of Joash are recorded in the chronicles of Judah.

verses.text: His servants arose, and made a conspiracy, and struck Joash at the house of Millo, on the way that goes down to Silla.
verses.explainer: Joash's own servants conspire against him and assassinate him at Beth-millo -- the king who survived Athaliah's massacre as an infant dies by assassination in his own court.

crossReferences.connection: The full account of Joash's decline after Jehoiada's death, including his murder of Zechariah

verses.text: For Jozacar the son of Shimeath, and Jehozabad the son of Shomer, his servants, struck him, and he died; and they buried him with his fathers in David’s city; and Amaziah his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: The assassins are named, and Joash is buried in Jerusalem but not in the royal tombs -- a final disgrace, denied burial with the kings despite his long reign.

explainer.summary: Israel's decline continues under Jehoahaz, who is oppressed by Hazael of Syria until God provides relief. Joash (Jehoash) of Israel succeeds him and visits the dying Elisha, who prophesies limited victory over Syria based on the king's halfhearted response to a symbolic action. Elisha dies, and even his bones possess resurrection power. The chapter records Israel's partial recovery from Syrian oppression.
themes: God's compassion despite persistent sin
The limits of halfhearted obedience
Elisha's death and continuing power
The slow recovery from divine judgment
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter marks the end of the Elisha era. The prophet's death leaves Israel without its greatest spiritual resource. Yet even in death, Elisha's bones raise the dead -- a final sign that God's power continues even when His prophets do not.

wordStudies.meaning: Deliverer or savior. God gives Israel a 'savior' despite their persistent sin, demonstrating that divine deliverance flows from God's compassion, not human merit. The identity of this savior is debated -- possibly an Assyrian king whose pressure on Syria relieved Israel.

The 'arrow of the LORD's deliverance.' Elisha uses arrows as prophetic symbols -- the number of times the king strikes the ground determines the number of victories. The symbolic action requires wholehearted participation to be fully effective.

verses.text: In the twenty-third year of Joash the son of Ahaziah, king of Judah, Jehoahaz the son of Jehu began to reign over Israel in Samaria for seventeen years.
verses.explainer: Jehoahaz son of Jehu reigns over Israel seventeen years -- the Jehu dynasty continues but under severe Syrian oppression.

verses.text: He did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight, and followed the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, with which he made Israel to sin. He didn’t depart from it.
verses.explainer: He does evil, following the sins of Jeroboam -- the standard condemnation for every northern king.

verses.text: Yahweh’s anger burned against Israel, and he delivered them into the hand of Hazael king of Syria, and into the hand of Benhadad the son of Hazael, continually.
verses.explainer: God gives Israel into Hazael's and Ben-hadad's hand throughout Jehoahaz's reign -- sustained military oppression as divine judgment for persistent idolatry.

crossReferences.connection: The fulfillment of Elisha's prophecy about Hazael's violence against Israel

verses.text: Jehoahaz begged Yahweh, and Yahweh listened to him; for he saw the oppression of Israel, how the king of Syria oppressed them.
verses.explainer: Jehoahaz prays to God, and God listens because He sees Israel's oppression under Syria -- even a king whose reign is condemned can find God responsive to genuine prayer.

thenVsNow.then: God responds to prayer even from those whose overall pattern of life is unfaithful, demonstrating that divine mercy is not proportional to human merit.
thenVsNow.now: God responds to prayer even from those whose overall pattern of life is unfaithful, demonstrating that divine mercy is not proportional to human merit.

verses.text: (Yahweh gave Israel a savior, so that they went out from under the hand of the Syrians; and the children of Israel lived in their tents as before.
verses.explainer: God gives Israel a savior who delivers them from Syrian power -- the identity of this deliverer is uncertain but may be the Assyrian king whose pressure on Syria diverted their attention from Israel.

verses.text: Nevertheless they didn’t depart from the sins of the house of Jeroboam, with which he made Israel to sin, but walked in them; and the Asherah also remained in Samaria.)
verses.explainer: Despite deliverance, Israel does not depart from Jeroboam's sins, and the Asherah pole remains in Samaria -- deliverance without repentance, relief without reform.

verses.text: For he didn’t leave to Jehoahaz of the people any more than fifty horsemen, and ten chariots, and ten thousand footmen; for the king of Syria destroyed them, and made them like the dust in threshing.
verses.explainer: Hazael reduces Israel's army to fifty horsemen, ten chariots, and ten thousand foot soldiers -- virtual destruction of Israel's military capacity.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Jehoahaz, and all that he did, and his might, aren’t they written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel?
verses.explainer: The summary formula for Jehoahaz's reign.

verses.text: Jehoahaz slept with his fathers; and they buried him in Samaria; and Joash his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Jehoahaz dies and his son Joash succeeds him.

verses.text: In the thirty-seventh year of Joash king of Judah, Jehoash the son of Jehoahaz began to reign over Israel in Samaria for sixteen years.
verses.explainer: Joash (Jehoash) of Israel begins his sixteen-year reign.

verses.text: He did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight. He didn’t depart from all the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, with which he made Israel to sin; but he walked in them.
verses.explainer: He does evil, following Jeroboam's sins -- the unchanging verdict for northern kings.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Joash, and all that he did, and his might with which he fought against Amaziah king of Judah, aren’t they written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel?
verses.explainer: The summary of Joash's reign, including his war with Amaziah of Judah.

verses.text: Joash slept with his fathers; and Jeroboam sat on his throne. Joash was buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel.
verses.explainer: Joash dies and Jeroboam II succeeds him on Israel's throne.

verses.text: Now Elisha became sick with the illness of which he died; and Joash the king of Israel came down to him, and wept over him, and said, “My father, my father, the chariots of Israel and its horsemen!”
verses.explainer: Elisha becomes ill with the sickness from which he will die, and Joash visits him weeping: 'My father, my father! The chariots of Israel and its horsemen!' -- the same words Elisha spoke when Elijah departed.

crossReferences.connection: Elisha used these identical words when Elijah departed, creating a chain of prophetic succession and loss

verses.text: Elisha said to him, “Take bow and arrows”; and he took bow and arrows for himself.
verses.explainer: Elisha tells the king to take a bow and arrows -- the dying prophet's final act is a prophetic action predicting Israel's military future.

verses.text: He said to the king of Israel, “Put your hand on the bow”; and he put his hand on it. Elisha laid his hands on the king’s hands.
verses.explainer: Elisha places his hands on the king's hands as he draws the bow -- the prophet's hands on the king's hands symbolize divine power working through human action.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The prophet's hands covering the king's hands while shooting symbolized the transfer of divine power into the military action. The arrow became more than a weapon -- it became a prophetic symbol.

verses.text: He said, “Open the window eastward”; and he opened it. Then Elisha said, “Shoot!” and he shot. He said, “Yahweh’s arrow of victory, even the arrow of victory over Syria; for you will strike the Syrians in Aphek, until you have consumed them.”
verses.explainer: Elisha tells the king to open the east window and shoot, declaring 'The arrow of the LORD's deliverance from Syria!' -- the arrow symbolizes victory over Syria.

verses.text: He said, “Take the arrows”; and he took them. He said to the king of Israel, “Strike the ground”; and he struck three times, and stopped.
verses.explainer: Elisha tells the king to strike the ground with the arrows, and the king strikes three times and stops -- an act that will determine the completeness of Israel's victory.

verses.text: The man of God was angry with him, and said, “You should have struck five or six times. Then you would have struck Syria until you had consumed it; whereas now you will strike Syria just three times.”
verses.explainer: Elisha is angry: the king should have struck five or six times, ensuring complete victory. Now he will defeat Syria only three times -- the number of symbolic strikes limits the actual victories.

thenVsNow.then: The principle that the intensity of our spiritual engagement determines the completeness of God's work in our lives is illustrated by this episode -- halfhearted faith produces limited results.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that the intensity of our spiritual engagement determines the completeness of God's work in our lives is illustrated by this episode -- halfhearted faith produces limited results.

verses.text:
Elisha died, and they buried him.
Now the bands of the Moabites invaded the land at the coming in of the year.
verses.explainer: Elisha dies and is buried. Moabite raiders enter the land every spring -- the loss of the prophet is immediately felt as enemies grow bolder.

verses.text: As they were burying a man, behold, they saw a band of raiders; and they threw the man into Elisha’s tomb. As soon as the man touched Elisha’s bones, he revived, and stood up on his feet.
verses.explainer: Men burying a body hastily throw it into Elisha's tomb when they see raiders. When the body touches Elisha's bones, the dead man revives and stands up -- resurrection power persists even in the prophet's bones.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: This is the only instance in Scripture of resurrection through contact with a dead person's remains. It does not endorse relic veneration but demonstrates that God's power associated with His prophet transcends death itself.

thenVsNow.then: The miracle of Elisha's bones points beyond itself to the resurrection hope that pervades Scripture -- death does not have the final word.
thenVsNow.now: The miracle of Elisha's bones points beyond itself to the resurrection hope that pervades Scripture -- death does not have the final word.

verses.text: Hazael king of Syria oppressed Israel all the days of Jehoahaz.
verses.explainer: Hazael oppresses Israel throughout Jehoahaz's reign -- the sustained pressure of divine judgment through foreign oppression.

verses.text: But Yahweh was gracious to them, and had compassion on them, and had respect for them, because of his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and would not destroy them, and he didn’t cast them from his presence as yet.
verses.explainer: But the LORD is gracious, compassionate, and unwilling to destroy them because of His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob -- the patriarchal covenant, not the people's merit, sustains God's patience.

annotations.word: because of his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob

crossReferences.connection: God remembered His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob when Israel groaned in Egypt -- the same covenantal basis for mercy

verses.text: Hazael king of Syria died; and Benhadad his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Hazael dies and Ben-hadad his son succeeds him -- the change of Syrian leadership creates an opportunity for Israel's recovery.

verses.text: Jehoash the son of Jehoahaz took again out of the hand of Benhadad the son of Hazael the cities which he had taken out of the hand of Jehoahaz his father by war. Joash struck him three times, and recovered the cities of Israel.
verses.explainer: Joash defeats Ben-hadad three times, exactly as Elisha prophesied, and recovers the Israelite cities -- three victories, no more and no less, precisely matching the three strikes on the ground.

crossReferences.connection: Exactly three victories, corresponding to the three times the king struck the ground

explainer.summary: Amaziah of Judah executes his father's assassins but spares their children, following Mosaic law. He defeats Edom but then foolishly challenges Joash of Israel, who defeats him, plunders Jerusalem, and breaks down part of its wall. After Amaziah's assassination, his son Azariah succeeds him. In the north, Jeroboam II restores Israel's borders to their greatest extent since Solomon, though the narrator gives him minimal attention.
themes: Pride after victory leading to defeat
The law of individual responsibility
Israel's last period of prosperity under Jeroboam II
The disconnect between political success and spiritual faithfulness
explainer.contextInBook: Jeroboam II's reign represents the last golden age of the northern kingdom -- politically powerful but spiritually hollow. The prophets Amos and Hosea ministered during this period, condemning the social injustice that prosperity produced.

wordStudies.meaning: A thorn bush or thistle. Joash's fable comparing Amaziah to a thistle that dares challenge a cedar is a devastating put-down, suggesting Judah is a prickly weed that should not presume to compete with a great tree (Israel).

To restore territorial boundaries. Jeroboam II restored Israel's borders from Lebo-hamath to the Dead Sea, achieving the greatest territorial extent since Solomon. Yet the narrator devotes only a few verses to this remarkable achievement.

verses.text: In the second year of Joash son of Joahaz king of Israel Amaziah the son of Joash king of Judah began to reign.
verses.explainer: Amaziah son of Joash becomes king of Judah in the second year of Joash of Israel.

verses.text: He was twenty-five years old when he began to reign; and he reigned twenty-nine years in Jerusalem. His mother’s name was Jehoaddin of Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: He is twenty-five when he begins and reigns twenty-nine years in Jerusalem.

verses.text: He did that which was right in Yahweh’s eyes, yet not like David his father. He did according to all that Joash his father had done.
verses.explainer: He does right in God's eyes but not like David -- a qualified approval that recognizes real but limited faithfulness.

verses.text: However the high places were not taken away. The people still sacrificed and burned incense in the high places.
verses.explainer: The high places are not removed -- the standard limitation noted for nearly every Judahite king.

verses.text: As soon as the kingdom was established in his hand, he killed his servants who had slain the king his father,
verses.explainer: When the kingdom is firmly in his hand, Amaziah executes the servants who assassinated his father -- waiting until he is secure before acting against powerful conspirators.

thenVsNow.then: Political timing in pursuing justice requires waiting until one's authority is established -- premature action can destabilize the very power needed to act.
thenVsNow.now: Political timing in pursuing justice requires waiting until one's authority is established -- premature action can destabilize the very power needed to act.

verses.text: but the children of the murderers he didn’t put to death; according to that which is written in the book of the law of Moses, as Yahweh commanded, saying, “The fathers shall not be put to death for the children, nor the children be put to death for the fathers; but every man shall die for his own sin.”
verses.explainer: He does not kill the assassins' children, following the law of Moses that fathers shall not be put to death for children or children for fathers -- a remarkable application of the principle of individual responsibility.

crossReferences.connection: The law of individual responsibility that Amaziah follows, prohibiting collective family punishment

verses.text: He killed ten thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt, and took Sela by war, and called its name Joktheel, to this day.
verses.explainer: Amaziah defeats ten thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt and captures Sela -- a significant military victory that restores Judah's control over southern trade routes.

verses.text: Then Amaziah sent messengers to Jehoash, the son of Jehoahaz son of Jehu, king of Israel, saying, “Come, let us look one another in the face.”
verses.explainer: Flushed with victory, Amaziah challenges Joash of Israel to battle: 'Come, let us face one another' -- pride after defeating Edom leads him to overestimate his military power.

thenVsNow.then: Success in one area often breeds dangerous overconfidence in another. Amaziah's Edomite victory made him think he could take on a much stronger opponent.
thenVsNow.now: Success in one area often breeds dangerous overconfidence in another. Amaziah's Edomite victory made him think he could take on a much stronger opponent.

verses.text: Jehoash the king of Israel sent to Amaziah king of Judah, saying, “The thistle that was in Lebanon sent to the cedar that was in Lebanon, saying, ‘Give your daughter to my son as wife. Then a wild animal that was in Lebanon passed by, and trampled down the thistle.
verses.explainer: Joash responds with a fable: a thistle demanded a cedar's daughter in marriage, and a wild beast trampled the thistle -- a devastating comparison, calling Amaziah a weed that will be crushed.

verses.text: You have indeed struck Edom, and your heart has lifted you up. Enjoy the glory of it, and stay at home; for why should you meddle to your harm, that you fall, even you, and Judah with you?’”
verses.explainer: Joash warns Amaziah to enjoy his Edomite victory and stay home, because picking a fight with Israel will bring disaster on both Amaziah and Judah -- sound advice that pride prevents from being heard.

verses.text: But Amaziah would not listen. So Jehoash king of Israel went up; and he and Amaziah king of Judah looked one another in the face at Beth Shemesh, which belongs to Judah.
verses.explainer: Amaziah refuses to listen, and they meet in battle at Beth-shemesh in Judah's territory -- the battle takes place on Judahite soil, meaning Israel is the aggressor after Amaziah's challenge.

verses.text: Judah was defeated by Israel; and each man fled to his tent.
verses.explainer: Judah is defeated and every man flees home -- total military collapse, vindicating Joash's warning.

verses.text: Jehoash king of Israel took Amaziah king of Judah, the son of Jehoash the son of Ahaziah, at Beth Shemesh, and came to Jerusalem, and broke down the wall of Jerusalem from the gate of Ephraim to the corner gate, four hundred cubits.
verses.explainer: Joash captures Amaziah and marches to Jerusalem, breaking down 600 feet of the city wall -- deliberately humiliating Judah's defenses without fully destroying the city.

verses.text: He took all the gold and silver, and all the vessels that were found in Yahweh’s house and in the treasures of the king’s house, the hostages also, and returned to Samaria.
verses.explainer: Joash takes all the gold, silver, and temple vessels he can find, plus hostages, and returns to Samaria -- looting Jerusalem's treasures, a preview of what Babylon will do a century and a half later.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Jehoash which he did, and his might, and how he fought with Amaziah king of Judah, aren’t they written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel?
verses.explainer: The summary formula for Joash of Israel.

verses.text: Jehoash slept with his fathers, and was buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel; and Jeroboam his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Joash of Israel dies and Jeroboam II succeeds him.

verses.text: Amaziah the son of Joash king of Judah lived after the death of Jehoash son of Jehoahaz king of Israel fifteen years.
verses.explainer: Amaziah lives fifteen years after Joash of Israel's death -- a long but diminished reign after the humiliation of defeat.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Amaziah, aren’t they written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?
verses.explainer: The summary formula for Amaziah's reign.

verses.text: They made a conspiracy against him in Jerusalem, and he fled to Lachish; but they sent after him to Lachish, and killed him there.
verses.explainer: A conspiracy forms against Amaziah in Jerusalem, and he flees to Lachish but is followed and killed there -- the second Judahite king in succession to be assassinated.

verses.text: They brought him on horses, and he was buried at Jerusalem with his fathers in David’s city.
verses.explainer: His body is brought back to Jerusalem on horses and buried with his fathers in the city of David.

verses.text: All the people of Judah took Azariah, who was sixteen years old, and made him king in the place of his father Amaziah.
verses.explainer: The people make his sixteen-year-old son Azariah (Uzziah) king in his place -- popular choice restoring the Davidic succession.

verses.text: He built Elath, and restored it to Judah. After that the king slept with his fathers.
verses.explainer: Azariah rebuilds Elath and restores it to Judah -- recovering the Red Sea port that gave Judah access to international trade routes.

verses.text: In the fifteenth year of Amaziah the son of Joash king of Judah, Jeroboam the son of Joash king of Israel began to reign in Samaria for forty-one years.
verses.explainer: Jeroboam II son of Joash begins his forty-one-year reign over Israel -- the longest and most powerful northern reign, yet the narrator gives him minimal space.

verses.text: He did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight. He didn’t depart from all the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, with which he made Israel to sin.
verses.explainer: He does evil, following Jeroboam's sins -- despite political greatness, the spiritual verdict remains negative.

verses.text: He restored the border of Israel from the entrance of Hamath to the sea of the Arabah, according to Yahweh, the God of Israel’s word, which he spoke by his servant Jonah the son of Amittai, the prophet, who was from Gath Hepher.
verses.explainer: Jeroboam restores Israel's border from Lebo-hamath to the Sea of the Arabah, fulfilling Jonah's prophecy -- Israel's greatest territorial extent since Solomon, prophesied by the same Jonah of the famous fish story.

crossReferences.connection: The same Jonah who prophesied Israel's restoration here is later sent to Nineveh, the capital of the empire that would eventually destroy Israel

verses.text: For Yahweh saw the affliction of Israel, that it was very bitter; for all, slave and free, and there was no helper for Israel.
verses.explainer: God sees Israel's bitter affliction, with no one to help, and does not intend to blot out Israel's name -- compassion motivates the territorial restoration, not the king's merit.

thenVsNow.then: Political prosperity often occurs not because of a nation's moral deserving but because of God's compassion and unfulfilled purposes. Success is not necessarily a sign of divine approval.
thenVsNow.now: Political prosperity often occurs not because of a nation's moral deserving but because of God's compassion and unfulfilled purposes. Success is not necessarily a sign of divine approval.

verses.text: Yahweh didn’t say that he would blot out the name of Israel from under the sky; but he saved them by the hand of Jeroboam the son of Joash.
verses.explainer: The LORD does not say He will blot out the name of Israel, so He saves them through Jeroboam -- God uses even an unfaithful king as an instrument of temporary deliverance.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Jeroboam, and all that he did, and his might, how he fought, and how he recovered Damascus, and Hamath, which had belonged to Judah, for Israel, aren’t they written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel?
verses.explainer: The summary formula mentioning Jeroboam's recovery of Damascus and Hamath -- territories not controlled by Israel since Solomon's time, given only passing mention.

verses.text: Jeroboam slept with his fathers, even with the kings of Israel; and Zechariah his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Jeroboam dies and Zechariah his son succeeds him -- the fourth generation of Jehu's dynasty, the limit God promised.

explainer.summary: A rapid survey of multiple kings in both kingdoms. In Judah, Azariah (Uzziah) reigns well but is struck with leprosy for entering the temple. In Israel, the Jehu dynasty ends with Zechariah's assassination after only six months, followed by a cascade of assassinations: Shallum, Menahem, Pekahiah, and Pekah. The Assyrian threat grows as Tiglath-pileser III begins intervening in Israelite affairs.
themes: The accelerating collapse of the northern kingdom
The rise of Assyria as an existential threat
Political instability as a sign of divine judgment
The contrast between Judah's relative stability and Israel's chaos
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 15 accelerates the narrative pace dramatically, covering roughly fifty years in thirty-eight verses. The rapid succession of kings, most of them assassinated, shows the northern kingdom spiraling toward its inevitable destruction.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The skin disease that strikes Azariah (Uzziah) for
wordStudies.meaning: The skin disease that strikes Azariah (Uzziah) for presuming to offer incense in the temple. His leprosy forces him to live in isolation while his son Jotham governs. The disease functions as visible divine judgment for overstepping the boundary between royal and priestly authority.
A conspiracy or plot. Used repeatedly in this chapter as king after king is assassinated. The word's repetition creates a rhythm of violence that characterizes the northern kingdom's final decades.

verses.text: In the twenty-seventh year of Jeroboam king of Israel, Azariah son of Amaziah king of Judah began to reign.
verses.explainer: Azariah (Uzziah) son of Amaziah becomes king of Judah in Jeroboam II's twenty-seventh year.

verses.text: He was sixteen years old when he began to reign, and he reigned fifty-two years in Jerusalem. His mother’s name was Jecoliah of Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: He is sixteen when he becomes king and reigns fifty-two years -- the longest reign in Judah's history, indicating exceptional divine favor.

verses.text: He did that which was right in Yahweh’s eyes, according to all that his father Amaziah had done.
verses.explainer: He does right in God's eyes, following his father Amaziah -- a generally positive evaluation.

verses.text: However the high places were not taken away. The people still sacrificed and burned incense in the high places.
verses.explainer: The high places are not removed -- the recurring limitation.

verses.text: Yahweh struck the king, so that he was a leper to the day of his death, and lived in a separate house. Jotham, the king’s son was over the household, judging the people of the land.
verses.explainer: The LORD strikes Azariah with leprosy until his death, and he lives in a separate house while his son Jotham governs -- the Chronicler explains this was punishment for attempting to offer incense in the temple.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The king's leprosy demonstrated that royal power had limits defined by God. Even the most powerful king could not usurp priestly functions. This principle of separated powers -- royal and priestly -- was foundational to Israelite governance.

crossReferences.connection: The detailed account of Uzziah's presumptuous entry into the temple and the leprosy that resulted

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Azariah, and all that he did, aren’t they written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?
verses.explainer: The summary formula for Azariah.

verses.text: Azariah slept with his fathers; and they buried him with his fathers in David’s city: and Jotham his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Azariah dies and Jotham his son succeeds him.

verses.text: In the thirty-eighth year of Azariah king of Judah, Zechariah the son of Jeroboam reigned over Israel in Samaria six months.
verses.explainer: Zechariah son of Jeroboam II reigns six months -- the fourth and final generation of Jehu's dynasty, as God promised.

crossReferences.connection: God's promise that Jehu's sons would sit on the throne to the fourth generation, now exhausted

verses.text: He did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight, as his fathers had done. He didn’t depart from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, with which he made Israel to sin.
verses.explainer: Zechariah does evil, following Jeroboam's sins -- the unchanging evaluation for the dynasty's final member.

verses.text: Shallum the son of Jabesh conspired against him, and struck him before the people, and killed him, and reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Shallum conspires against Zechariah and kills him publicly, seizing the throne -- the violent end of the longest dynasty in the northern kingdom.

thenVsNow.then: The public nature of the assassination indicates how completely the Jehu dynasty had lost popular support, despite its long tenure.
thenVsNow.now: The public nature of the assassination indicates how completely the Jehu dynasty had lost popular support, despite its long tenure.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Zechariah, behold, they are written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel.
verses.explainer: Zechariah's brief record is noted.

verses.text: This was Yahweh’s word which he spoke to Jehu, saying, “Your sons to the fourth generation shall sit on the throne of Israel.” So it came to pass.
verses.explainer: This fulfills God's word to Jehu: 'Your sons to the fourth generation shall sit on the throne' -- the narrator connects the political event to the divine promise.

crossReferences.connection: The specific promise now fulfilled: four generations and no more

verses.text: Shallum the son of Jabesh began to reign in the thirty-ninth year of Uzziah king of Judah, and he reigned for a month in Samaria.
verses.explainer: Shallum reigns only one month before being killed -- the second-shortest reign in Israel's history, barely qualifying as a reign at all.

verses.text: Menahem the son of Gadi went up from Tirzah, came to Samaria, struck Shallum the son of Jabesh in Samaria, killed him, and reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Menahem comes from Tirzah and kills Shallum in Samaria -- the third king in rapid succession to seize power through assassination.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Shallum, and his conspiracy which he made, behold, they are written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel.
verses.explainer: Shallum's deeds and conspiracy are recorded.

verses.text: Then Menahem attacked Tiphsah, and all who were in it, and its border areas, from Tirzah. He attacked it because they didn’t open their gates to him, and he ripped up all their women who were with child.
verses.explainer: Menahem attacks Tiphsah because it did not open its gates, and he rips open all the pregnant women -- extreme violence against his own people who resist his authority.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Ripping open pregnant women was the most condemned atrocity in ancient warfare (Amos 1:13). That an Israelite king commits this against Israelite cities shows the kingdom has become as brutal as the nations it was supposed to be different from.

verses.text: In the thirty ninth year of Azariah king of Judah, Menahem the son of Gadi began to reign over Israel for ten years in Samaria.
verses.explainer: Menahem reigns ten years -- a relatively stable period bought through brutal suppression of opposition and Assyrian vassalage.

verses.text: He did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight. He didn’t depart all his days from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, with which he made Israel to sin.
verses.explainer: He does evil, following Jeroboam's sins.

verses.text: Pul the king of Assyria came against the land, and Menahem gave Pul one thousand talents of silver, that his hand might be with him to confirm the kingdom in his hand.
verses.explainer: Tiglath-pileser (Pul) of Assyria invades, and Menahem pays him a thousand talents of silver to confirm his rule -- buying Assyrian support to secure his throne.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Tiglath-pileser III's appearance in 2 Kings marks a turning point. His policy of deportation and provincial reorganization would eventually destroy the northern kingdom. Menahem's tribute was the first of Israel's payments to the empire that would consume it.

verses.text: Menahem exacted the money from Israel, even from all the mighty men of wealth, from each man fifty shekels of silver, to give to the king of Assyria. So the king of Assyria turned back, and didn’t stay there in the land.
verses.explainer: Menahem extracts the silver from wealthy Israelites at fifty shekels each -- crushing taxation to pay foreign tribute, the same complaint that split the kingdom under Rehoboam.

thenVsNow.then: The irony is profound: the northern kingdom was founded on a rebellion against Solomon's heavy taxation, and now it taxes its own people even more heavily to pay foreign tribute.
thenVsNow.now: The irony is profound: the northern kingdom was founded on a rebellion against Solomon's heavy taxation, and now it taxes its own people even more heavily to pay foreign tribute.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Menahem, and all that he did, aren’t they written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel?
verses.explainer: Menahem's summary formula.

verses.text: Menahem slept with his fathers, and Pekahiah his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Menahem dies naturally -- one of the few northern kings to die in bed -- and his son Pekahiah succeeds him.

verses.text: In the fiftieth year of Azariah king of Judah, Pekahiah the son of Menahem began to reign over Israel in Samaria for two years.
verses.explainer: Pekahiah reigns two years.

verses.text: He did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight. He didn’t depart from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, with which he made Israel to sin.
verses.explainer: He does evil, following Jeroboam's sins.

verses.text: Pekah the son of Remaliah, his captain, conspired against him and attacked him in Samaria, in the fortress of the king’s house, with Argob and Arieh; and with him were fifty men of the Gileadites. He killed him, and reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Pekah son of Remaliah, one of his officers, conspires and kills him in the royal palace with fifty Gileadites -- another military coup, this time by an officer with Transjordanian support.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Pekahiah, and all that he did, behold, they are written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel.
verses.explainer: Pekahiah's summary formula.

verses.text: In the fifty-second year of Azariah king of Judah, Pekah the son of Remaliah began to reign over Israel in Samaria for twenty years.
verses.explainer: Pekah reigns twenty years -- a long reign for a usurper, but his anti-Assyrian policies will prove catastrophic for the northern kingdom.

verses.text: He did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight. He didn’t depart from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, with which he made Israel to sin.
verses.explainer: He does evil, following Jeroboam's sins.

verses.text: In the days of Pekah king of Israel, Tiglath Pileser king of Assyria came and took Ijon, Abel Beth Maacah, Janoah, Kedesh, Hazor, Gilead, and Galilee, all the land of Naphtali; and he carried them captive to Assyria.
verses.explainer: Tiglath-pileser of Assyria conquers major portions of Israel -- Ijon, Abel-beth-maacah, Janoah, Kedesh, Hazor, Gilead, Galilee, Naphtali -- and deports the population to Assyria.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Tiglath-pileser III's deportation policy was designed to break ethnic and national identities. Conquered peoples were scattered throughout the empire, making organized resistance impossible. This first deportation removed most of northern Israel's population.

thenVsNow.then: The Assyrian deportation policy of scattering conquered peoples was history's first systematic ethnic cleansing, destroying national identities by dispersing populations across a vast empire.
thenVsNow.now: The Assyrian deportation policy of scattering conquered peoples was history's first systematic ethnic cleansing, destroying national identities by dispersing populations across a vast empire.

verses.text: Hoshea the son of Elah made a conspiracy against Pekah the son of Remaliah, attacked him, killed him, and reigned in his place, in the twentieth year of Jotham the son of Uzziah.
verses.explainer: Hoshea conspires against Pekah and kills him, seizing the throne in Jotham's twentieth year -- the last king of Israel gains power through yet another assassination.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Pekah, and all that he did, behold, they are written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel.
verses.explainer: Pekah's summary formula.

verses.text: In the second year of Pekah the son of Remaliah king of Israel, Jotham the son of Uzziah king of Judah began to reign.
verses.explainer: Jotham son of Azariah (Uzziah) becomes king of Judah.

verses.text: He was twenty-five years old when he began to reign, and he reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem. His mother’s name was Jerusha the daughter of Zadok.
verses.explainer: He is twenty-five when he begins and reigns sixteen years.

verses.text: He did that which was right in Yahweh’s eyes. He did according to all that his father Uzziah had done.
verses.explainer: He does right, following his father Azariah -- another positive evaluation for a Judahite king.

verses.text: However the high places were not taken away. The people still sacrificed and burned incense in the high places. He built the upper gate of Yahweh’s house.
verses.explainer: The high places are not removed. He builds the Upper Gate of the temple -- contributing to the temple's infrastructure.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Jotham, and all that he did, aren’t they written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?
verses.explainer: Jotham's summary formula.

verses.text: In those days, Yahweh began to send Rezin the king of Syria and Pekah the son of Remaliah against Judah.
verses.explainer: During Jotham's reign, God begins sending Rezin of Syria and Pekah of Israel against Judah -- the beginning of the Syro-Ephraimite crisis that will define Ahaz's reign.

crossReferences.connection: Isaiah's response to the Syro-Ephraimite threat, offering Ahaz the sign of Immanuel

verses.text: Jotham slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in his father David’s city, and Ahaz his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Jotham dies and Ahaz his son succeeds him.

explainer.summary: Ahaz of Judah is one of the worst kings in Judah's history. He practices child sacrifice, replaces the bronze altar with a copy of a pagan altar from Damascus, and dismantles temple furnishings to pay tribute to Assyria. Under pressure from the Syro-Ephraimite coalition, he appeals to Assyria for help, becoming a vassal and importing Assyrian religious practices.
themes: Child sacrifice as the ultimate apostasy
Choosing political alliance over trust in God
The corruption of worship through syncretism
Assyrian domination as divine judgment
explainer.contextInBook: Ahaz represents Judah's lowest spiritual point before the eventual reforms of Hezekiah. His voluntary submission to Assyria and import of pagan worship practices undoes generations of partial faithfulness, setting the stage for the dramatic contrast with his son Hezekiah.

wordStudies.transliteration: Made his son pass through fire (he'evir)
wordStudies.translatedAs: To cause to pass through -- a euphemism for child
wordStudies.meaning: To cause to pass through -- a euphemism for child sacrifice, specifically associated with the worship of Molech. This practice, the most condemned act in the Old Testament, represents the absolute inversion of Israelite values.

A model, plan, or pattern. The same word used for the pattern of the tabernacle shown to Moses (Exodus 25:9) is used for the pagan altar Ahaz copies from Damascus -- a devastating contrast between divinely revealed design and imported paganism.

verses.text: In the seventeenth year of Pekah the son of Remaliah, Ahaz the son of Jotham king of Judah began to reign.
verses.explainer: Ahaz son of Jotham becomes king in Pekah's seventeenth year.

verses.text: Ahaz was twenty years old when he began to reign, and he reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem. He didn’t do that which was right in Yahweh his God’s eyes, like David his father.
verses.explainer: He is twenty when he begins and reigns sixteen years, but unlike David his father, he does not do right -- an explicit comparison to David that condemns Ahaz's entire reign.

verses.text: But he walked in the way of the kings of Israel, yes, and made his son to pass through the fire, according to the abominations of the nations whom Yahweh cast out from before the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: He walks in the ways of Israel's kings and makes his son pass through fire -- child sacrifice, the most abhorrent practice in the ancient world, adopted by a king of Judah.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Child sacrifice was practiced at the Topheth in the Valley of Hinnom (Gehenna), south of Jerusalem. This valley later became the symbol for hell in Jewish and Christian tradition, forever associated with the horror of Ahaz's and Manasseh's practices.

crossReferences.connection: The explicit prohibition against making children pass through fire, one of the 'abominations of the nations'

verses.text: He sacrificed and burned incense in the high places, on the hills, and under every green tree.
verses.explainer: He sacrifices and burns incense on high places, hills, and under every green tree -- comprehensive idolatry practiced throughout the landscape.

verses.text: Then Rezin king of Syria and Pekah son of Remaliah king of Israel came up to Jerusalem to wage war. They besieged Ahaz, but could not overcome him.
verses.explainer: Rezin of Syria and Pekah of Israel attack Jerusalem but cannot conquer it -- the Syro-Ephraimite crisis, the background to Isaiah 7.

crossReferences.connection: Isaiah's ministry during this crisis, offering Ahaz the sign of Immanuel

thenVsNow.then: The Syro-Ephraimite crisis presented Ahaz with a choice: trust God's promise through Isaiah or seek human military alliance. He chose Assyria over God.
thenVsNow.now: The Syro-Ephraimite crisis presented Ahaz with a choice: trust God's promise through Isaiah or seek human military alliance. He chose Assyria over God.

verses.text: At that time Rezin king of Syria recovered Elath to Syria, and drove the Jews from Elath; and the Syrians came to Elath, and lived there, to this day.
verses.explainer: Rezin recovers Elath for Syria and expels the Judahites -- Judah loses its Red Sea port, cutting off southern trade routes.

verses.text: So Ahaz sent messengers to Tiglath Pileser king of Assyria, saying, “I am your servant and your son. Come up and save me out of the hand of the king of Syria, and out of the hand of the king of Israel, who rise up against me.”
verses.explainer: Ahaz sends a message to Tiglath-pileser of Assyria: 'I am your servant and son; come save me from Syria and Israel' -- voluntary vassal submission to a foreign empire.

annotations.word: I am your servant and your son

verses.text: Ahaz took the silver and gold that was found in Yahweh’s house, and in the treasures of the king’s house, and sent it for a present to the king of Assyria.
verses.explainer: Ahaz strips the temple and palace of silver and gold to send as a bribe to the Assyrian king -- sacred treasures used to purchase political survival.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Paying tribute to Assyria was not a one-time event but an ongoing obligation. Ahaz committed Judah to annual payments that would drain the national treasury for generations.

verses.text: The king of Assyria listened to him; and the king of Assyria went up against Damascus, and took it, and carried its people captive to Kir, and killed Rezin.
verses.explainer: Assyria conquers Damascus, deports its population, and kills Rezin -- the Syrian threat is eliminated, but at the cost of Judah's independence.

verses.text: King Ahaz went to Damascus to meet Tiglath Pileser king of Assyria, and saw the altar that was at Damascus; and king Ahaz sent to Urijah the priest a drawing of the altar and plans to build it.
verses.explainer: Ahaz goes to Damascus to meet Tiglath-pileser and sees an altar there; he sends its design to Urijah the priest to replicate -- importing foreign worship into the temple.

thenVsNow.then: The willingness of religious leaders to comply with politically-motivated changes to worship practices is a recurring pattern whenever institutional religion serves political power.
thenVsNow.now: The willingness of religious leaders to comply with politically-motivated changes to worship practices is a recurring pattern whenever institutional religion serves political power.

verses.text: Urijah the priest built an altar. According to all that king Ahaz had sent from Damascus, so Urijah the priest made it for the coming of king Ahaz from Damascus.
verses.explainer: Urijah the priest builds the altar exactly according to Ahaz's design before the king returns -- the speed of compliance shows the priest's complete subordination to royal authority.

verses.text: When the king had come from Damascus, the king saw the altar; and the king came near to the altar, and offered on it.
verses.explainer: Ahaz returns and offers sacrifices on the new altar -- displacing God's altar with a foreign copy and personally inaugurating the syncretistic worship.

verses.text: He burned his burnt offering and his meal offering, poured his drink offering, and sprinkled the blood of his peace offerings on the altar.
verses.explainer: He offers burnt offerings, grain offerings, drink offerings, and splashes the blood of his peace offerings on the new altar -- a complete replacement of legitimate worship with foreign ritual.

verses.text: The bronze altar, which was before Yahweh, he brought from the front of the house, from between his altar and Yahweh’s house, and put it on the north side of his altar.
verses.explainer: He moves the original bronze altar away from its central position -- displacing the altar God commanded with a man-made substitute.

verses.text: King Ahaz commanded Urijah the priest, saying, “On the great altar burn the morning burnt offering, the evening meal offering, the king’s burnt offering, his meal offering, with the burnt offering of all the people of the land, their meal offering, and their drink offerings; and sprinkle on it all the blood of the burnt offering, and all the blood of the sacrifice; but the bronze altar will be for me to inquire by.”
verses.explainer: Ahaz commands Urijah to use the new altar for all regular sacrifices and the old bronze altar for his personal divination -- degrading the sacred altar to a tool for occult practices.

verses.text: Urijah the priest did so, according to all that king Ahaz commanded.
verses.explainer: Urijah does everything Ahaz commands -- the priest's complete submission to the king's religious innovations.

verses.text: King Ahaz cut off the panels of the bases, and removed the basin from off them, and took down the sea from off the bronze oxen that were under it, and put it on a pavement of stone.
verses.explainer: Ahaz cuts the panels of the movable stands and removes the basins, and takes the bronze sea off the bronze oxen -- dismantling Solomon's temple furnishings, undoing the glory of 1 Kings 7.

verses.text: He removed the covered way for the Sabbath that they had built in the house, and the king’s entry outside to Yahweh’s house, because of the king of Assyria.
verses.explainer: He removes the Sabbath canopy and the royal entrance because of the king of Assyria -- altering the temple to please the Assyrian overlord.

annotations.word: because of the king of Assyria

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Ahaz which he did, aren’t they written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?
verses.explainer: The summary formula for Ahaz.

verses.text: Ahaz slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in David’s city, and Hezekiah his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Ahaz dies and his son Hezekiah succeeds him -- the transition from one of Judah's worst kings to one of its best.

explainer.summary: The northern kingdom of Israel falls to Assyria after Hoshea, the last king, rebels against his Assyrian overlord. Samaria is captured after a three-year siege, and the population is deported. The narrator provides an extensive theological explanation for why the kingdom fell: persistent idolatry, rejection of prophetic warnings, and failure to keep the covenant. The Assyrians resettle foreigners in Israel's territory, creating a mixed worship system.
themes: The theological explanation for Israel's destruction
The catalog of covenant violations
God's patience and its limits
The origins of Samaritan religion
explainer.contextInBook: This is the theological climax of the northern kingdom's story. After twenty chapters of prophetic warnings and partial reforms, the narrator pauses to explain comprehensively why destruction came. The fall of Samaria in 722 BC is the defining event of the divided kingdom period.

wordStudies.meaning: To uncover, remove, or exile. The same word means both 'to reveal' and 'to deport' -- exile strips away everything, leaving a nation naked and exposed. Israel's deportation is both a political event and a theological unmasking.

To fear or revere. Used ironically of the resettled foreigners who 'feared the LORD' while also worshipping their own gods. Their fear was pragmatic (avoiding lion attacks) rather than genuine covenant devotion.

A decree or ordinance. The narrator contrasts following the 'statutes of the nations' with keeping the 'statutes of the LORD.' Israel chose the wrong set of rules, following pagan customs rather than divine law.

verses.text: In the twelfth year of Ahaz king of Judah, Hoshea the son of Elah began to reign in Samaria over Israel for nine years.
verses.explainer: Hoshea son of Elah becomes Israel's last king in Ahaz's twelfth year, reigning nine years -- the final chapter of the northern kingdom begins.

verses.text: He did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight, yet not as the kings of Israel who were before him.
verses.explainer: He does evil but not as much as previous kings -- a faint improvement that comes far too late to avert the accumulated judgment.

annotations.word: yet not as the kings of Israel who were before him

verses.text: Shalmaneser king of Assyria came up against him, and Hoshea became his servant, and brought him tribute.
verses.explainer: Shalmaneser of Assyria attacks, and Hoshea becomes his vassal, paying tribute -- Israel is reduced to a puppet state.

verses.text: The king of Assyria found conspiracy in Hoshea; for he had sent messengers to So king of Egypt, and offered no tribute to the king of Assyria, as he had done year by year. Therefore the king of Assyria seized him, and bound him in prison.
verses.explainer: The Assyrian king discovers Hoshea has sent secret envoys to Egypt and has stopped paying tribute, so he imprisons Hoshea -- rebellion against Assyria triggers the final invasion.

annotations.word: sent messengers to So king of Egypt

thenVsNow.then: Playing two superpowers against each other -- attempting to use Egypt against Assyria -- was a dangerous gamble that sealed Israel's fate. Small nations caught between great powers face impossible choices.
thenVsNow.now: Playing two superpowers against each other -- attempting to use Egypt against Assyria -- was a dangerous gamble that sealed Israel's fate. Small nations caught between great powers face impossible choices.

verses.text: Then the king of Assyria came up throughout all the land, went up to Samaria, and besieged it three years.
verses.explainer: The Assyrian king invades the entire land and besieges Samaria for three years -- a prolonged siege designed to starve the capital into submission.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Assyrian siege warfare was the most sophisticated in the ancient world. The three-year siege of Samaria involved complete encirclement, siege ramps, and a systematic starvation campaign against the fortified hilltop city.

verses.text: In the ninth year of Hoshea the king of Assyria took Samaria, and carried Israel away to Assyria, and placed them in Halah, and on the Habor, the river of Gozan, and in the cities of the Medes.
verses.explainer: In Hoshea's ninth year, Assyria captures Samaria and deports Israel to various locations in the Assyrian empire -- the northern kingdom ceases to exist as a political entity.

crossReferences.connection: Ahijah's prophecy centuries earlier that God would uproot Israel and scatter them beyond the Euphrates

verses.text: It was so because the children of Israel had sinned against Yahweh their God, who brought them up out of the land of Egypt from under the hand of Pharaoh king of Egypt, and had feared other gods,
verses.explainer: The narrator begins his theological explanation: this happened because Israel sinned against the LORD who brought them out of Egypt, and they feared other gods -- the fundamental covenant violation.

verses.text: and walked in the statutes of the nations whom Yahweh cast out from before the children of Israel, and of the kings of Israel, which they made.
verses.explainer: They followed the customs of the nations God drove out before them and the customs their kings introduced -- combining Canaanite and royal innovations to create a comprehensive system of apostasy.

verses.text: The children of Israel secretly did things that were not right against Yahweh their God; and they built high places for themselves in all their cities, from the tower of the watchmen to the fortified city;
verses.explainer: Israel secretly did things against the LORD that were not right, building high places in every town -- sin practiced covertly, attempting to worship Yahweh and idols simultaneously.

verses.text: and they set up for themselves pillars and Asherah poles on every high hill, and under every green tree;
